You poured out their opinions within it, and they did not hesitate, To show you that you were more foolish in what you brought! You sought to declare them sinners in their religion, But behold, the drinkers of wine surpassed the 'Farouq' in jurisprudence!
What is the virtue in this? I do not know.

They said: Stop! We have come with one sin, While you have come to us with three, not caring for them! So approach houses from their doors, O Umar, For the one who comes from the walls may be accused [of being a thief]! And seek permission from people to enter their homes, And do not enter a house without greeting it! And do not spy; this verse has been revealed, Forbidding it, so you did not remember its prohibitions!

He didn't do anything to them because they silenced him! ... Meaning the virtue here, the intent of this witness, is that this issue is known, such that it is versified by poets and mentioned in books. A known issue that he scaled the wall into the house without permission! And this issue is present, and we will see it repeat. We will see this issue: **that he attacks the houses of people, and enters the houses of people by force and without permission!**
So then, the issue of the attack on the house of Fatima becomes an issue within the sequence of this exact same context! The nature, the behavior, and what is the strange thing [about it]?
Segment 7: The Shaqshaqiyyah Sermon and the Assassination Plot of the Shura

Sheikh al-Ghizzi analyzes Imam Ali's own description of Umar's caliphate from the famous Shaqshaqiyyah Sermon, where the Imam describes it as a "harsh enclosure" ridden with errors and violence. The Sheikh then exposes the reality of Umar's six-man Shura (electoral council) upon his deathbed. Using Sunni sources like Tarikh al-Tabari and Al-Kamil fi al-Tarikh, he shows that Umar rigged the council by ordering the execution of any dissenting minority. If the council tied three-to-three, Umar explicitly commanded them to side with Abd al-Rahman ibn Awf and kill the opposing three—a directive Imam Ali knew was a targeted assassination plot against him, as Abd al-Rahman was his known rival.

Therefore, when we read in Nahj al-Balaghah, in the **Shaqshaqiyyah Sermon**, how the Commander of the Believers describes Umar ibn al-Khattab... He says: "So he passed it on to the son of al-Khattab after him."

So he placed it in a harsh enclosure (Hawzah Khashna')... whose wounding is severe (Yughlizu Kalmuha), its touch is rough, its stumblings are many, and the excuses for it are many. Its possessor is like the rider of a difficult camel (al-Sa'bah): if he pulls its reins tight, her nose is torn, and if he loosens it for her, she plunges into destruction!
This is Ali's description of Umar's caliphate and Umar's personality! ... Then the Imam says:

So I remained patient upon the length of the period and the severity of the ordeal. Until he went his way, he placed it in a group [the Shura]! He claimed that I was one of them! So, O Allah, and the Shura! When did doubt enter concerning me with the first of them, that I am now coupled with these equals?!

In the twenty-ninth volume of **Bihar al-Anwar**... Sheikh al-Mufid with his chain transmits... 'I heard the Commander of the Believers, Ali ibn Abi Talib, peace be upon him, addressing the people, so he said in his sermon:'
Then Umar perished, and made it a Shura, making me the sixth of six! Like the share of the grandmother! And he said: 'Kill the minority.' And he intended no one but me!
This is **Tarikh al-Tabari**. And this is the second volume... 'And he said to Suhayb:'

Stand over their heads! If five agree and choose a man, and one refuses, strike his head with the sword! And if four agree and two refuse, strike their heads! And if three agree on a man and three on a man, then make Abdullah ibn Umar the judge... If they do not accept the judgment of Abdullah ibn Umar, then be with the group in which is Abd al-Rahman ibn Awf, and kill the rest!

**He was guaranteeing that Abd al-Rahman ibn Awf would not vote for Ali, and kill the rest!** And this is the Shura! And this is the true democracy that they talk about in their gatherings! This is the Shura!

Umar was aware that Abd al-Rahman ibn Awf would be on the opposing side to Ali, prayers of Allah and His peace be upon him. So he said to them: "If they do not accept the judgment of Abdullah ibn Umar, then be with the group in which is Abd al-Rahman ibn Awf, and kill the rest." As the Master of Legatees said: "He said, kill the minority, and he intended no one but me."
Segment 8: The Confession in Sahih Muslim - "Liar, Sinful, Treacherous, and Dishonest"

Sheikh al-Ghizzi turns to one of the most authentic Sunni hadith collections, Sahih Muslim, to reveal Imam Ali's true view of Abu Bakr and Umar. He quotes Umar's own explicit confession during a dispute over the Prophet's estate, where Umar admits to Imam Ali and Al-Abbas that they both viewed Abu Bakr—and subsequently Umar himself—as a "liar, sinful, treacherous, and dishonest" man. The Sheikh emphasizes that this devastating admission in a primary Sunni text perfectly aligns with the Shia narrative of Imam Ali's profound opposition to the Caliphs. He then provides a comprehensive recap of the episode thus far, using these established facts to transition to his ultimate point: Umar's violent history with women.

Therefore, we are not surprised by what came mentioned in **Sahih Muslim**... Hadith number 4,593. And it is the hadith in which Umar ibn al-Khattab spoke and clarified the view of Ali and the view of Al-Abbas regarding Abu Bakr and regarding himself.

So Abu Bakr said: 'The Messenger of Allah said: We do not leave inheritance, what we leave is charity.' So you both saw him... 'You saw him as a liar, sinful, treacherous, and dishonest (Kadhiban, Athiman, Ghadiran, Kha'inan)!' ... Then he says: 'So you saw me as a liar, sinful, treacherous, and dishonest!'

A narration transmitted from Umar ibn al-Khattab himself! **He is the one who clarifies that Ali's view of Abu Bakr was that Abu Bakr was a liar, sinful, treacherous, and dishonest! And that Ali believed regarding Umar that he was a liar, sinful, treacherous, and dishonest!**

Then it becomes clear. These meanings are in complete agreement with the words of Ali present in the books of the Shia! And with these facts present in the books of the people as well, in their books.

All of this was an introduction... We spoke about the view of the Companions regarding him... And it passed upon us how he dealt in his transactions... how he scaled over the houses of people... what he did in cutting the tree at which the Allegiance of the Tree occurred...

The direction I want to reach in this speech: **How did Umar ibn al-Khattab deal with women in a general sense?** Because there are those who say that the Arabs disdained striking women. This is true, but does this speech apply to Umar?
Segment 9: The Assaults on Women - The Sister, the Daughter, and the Female Converts

Sheikh al-Ghizzi directly challenges the claim that Arab chivalry prevented Umar from striking women. By reading from Al-Kamil fi al-Tarikh and other Sunni texts, the Sheikh details a chilling, lifelong pattern of extreme violence against women. He recounts how Umar spied on his own sister, burst into her home unannounced, and struck her face so hard it caused a deep, bleeding gash. He then turns to the horrific pre-Islamic account of Umar burying his own innocent daughter alive, watching her brush the dust from his beard as he murdered her. The segment concludes with accounts of Umar systematically torturing early female Muslim converts, beating them until he was physically exhausted, proving that horrific violence against women was an ingrained, defining characteristic of his nature.

This is **Al-Kamil fi al-Tarikh**. This is the first volume... Let us see how Umar ibn al-Khattab deals with women. This speech is in the Jahiliyyah (days of ignorance), before Islam, before he became Muslim.

When it was said to him that your sister and her husband have entered the religion of Muhammad, he went angrily, spying on the house of his sister! ... This issue of entering houses without permission is an issue accompanying him constantly; the witnesses will come.

When he entered, he said: 'What is this low murmuring (Haynamah)?' ... And he struck his brother-in-law Sa'id ibn Zayd (Batasha bi-Khatanih). ... So his sister stood up to him to restrain him, to prevent Umar from striking her husband. So he struck her and gashed her (Shajjaha)!

**So he struck her and gashed her!** Meaning he struck her on her face and wounded her deeply. ... The point is that the issue of spying on houses is an old issue! And entering without permission is an old issue! This is in the time of Jahiliyyah. And this is his sister! **And striking women to the point of drawing blood is an old issue!**

And perhaps older than that is the known story that he buried his daughter alive! ... Abbas Mahmoud al-Aqqad, the Egyptian writer... mentions this issue, the issue of Umar burying his daughter alive. **And which, when he wanted to throw her alive into the grave, she was brushing the dust off his beard!**
Umar himself says:

As for the one that makes me weep: the story of the child whom I took out to the desert, and dug a grave for her. And when the dust came out of the grave, the child was brushing the dust off my beard! Then I threw her in alive!
**This is the root of the living graveyards! This is the root of the mass graves in which the living were buried!** This is the pre-Islamic root.

So if he acts this way with the child! And he acts this way with his sister! And even with the rest of the Muslim women... There is a report transmitted in Al-Sirah al-Nabawiyyah... **That Umar used to severely torture the slave girl of Banu Mu'ammil!** He would torture her, and after he became exhausted from the prolonged torture, he would leave her and say to her: 'I apologize to you, I only left you because I got tired!'
Umm Abdullah bint Abi Hathmah... said:

We were departing to the land of Abyssinia, fleeing from the torture. And 'Amir had gone for some of his needs, when Umar approached... So he said: 'Are you leaving, O Umm Abdullah?' ... When her husband 'Amir returned, she told him, 'If you had seen Umar and his softness!' He said: 'Do you hope for his Islam?' I said: 'Yes.' He said: 'He will not become Muslim until the donkey of al-Khattab becomes Muslim!'

He said: "He will not become Muslim." This is his nature, he has harshness and a lack of reason. "He will not become Muslim until the donkey of al-Khattab becomes Muslim!" Because of what he used to see of his harshness and severity against the Muslims.
Segment 10: The Rejection by Women - "Severe Against Women"

Sheikh al-Ghizzi examines the reputation Umar ibn al-Khattab held among the women of his own community. Quoting Al-Kamil fi al-Tarikh and Tarikh al-Tabari, the Sheikh recounts how Umar proposed to Umm Kulthum, the young daughter of Abu Bakr. She flatly rejected the Caliph, stating explicitly that he was known to lead a rough life and was "severe against women." Aisha, embarrassed by the rejection, had to employ Amr ibn al-'As to diplomatically dissuade Umar from the marriage. Another proposal to Umm Aban bint 'Utbah was similarly rejected, with her describing him as a man who "locks his door, withholds his good, enters frowning, and leaves frowning." The Sheikh stresses that this fear of Umar's domestic violence was widespread even among the highest echelons of the Caliphate.
This is **Al-Kamil fi al-Tarikh**, and this is the second volume... "And he proposed to Umm Kulthum, the daughter of Abu Bakr al-Siddiq, through Aisha."
So Umm Kulthum said: 'I have no need for him! He is rough in living, and severe against women!'

And these are the closest people to him! Meaning the family of Abu Bakr are the closest people to him... So Aisha sent for the shrewd one of the nation, Amr ibn al-'As. She told him of the matter, saying: 'I am embarrassed.'

So he came to Umar and said: ... 'But she is a young girl who grew up under the care of the Commander of the Believers [Abu Bakr] in softness and gentleness. And in you is harshness (Ghilzah)! And we fear you, and we cannot turn you back from any of your morals! So how will it be with her if she disobeys you in something and you assault her (Satawta biha)?!'

What did Umar say? Imagine Amr ibn al-'As and Umar ibn al-Khattab. What did Umar say? "So what about Aisha, when I have already spoken to her?" ... He said: "I will handle her for you." And he arranged the matter with Aisha.
Then the speech continues. He says:

And he [Umar] proposed to Umm Aban bint 'Utbah ibn Rabi'ah. So she hated him. She said: 'I do not want this one!' And she said: 'He locks his door, withholds his good, enters frowning, and leaves frowning!'
This is **Tarikh al-Tabari**... The exact same speech:
So Umm Kulthum said: 'I have no need for him.' So Aisha said: 'Do you reject the Commander of the Believers?' She said: 'Yes! He is rough in living, severe against women!'
A question for them: How did you succeed Muhammad in his offspring and in his only daughter Fatima?!
Segment 11: The Midnight Beating and the Forced Marriage of 'Atikah

Sheikh al-Ghizzi brings forward a disturbing narration from the Sunni authentic collections, Sunan Ibn Majah and Sunan Abi Dawud. The text details how Umar woke up in the middle of the night, left his guest, and proceeded to physically beat his wife. When he returned, he claimed he heard a hadith stating that "a man is not asked why he beats his wife." The Sheikh then presents another shocking historical account from Tabaqat Ibn Sa'd, revealing that Umar forced 'Atikah bint Zayd into marriage against her will, physically wrestling her to overpower her. This firmly establishes his extreme physical violence within his own household.
Let us go to a text from the texts. This text, this is **Sunan Ibn Majah**. And Sunan Ibn Majah is from the known Sihah (authentic books) among them... Hadith number 1,986.

From Al-Ash'ath ibn Qays, he said: 'I was a guest of Umar for a night. So when it was the middle of the night, he got up to his wife, beating her! And I intervened between them.' ... When he returned to his bed, he said to me: 'O Ash'ath, memorize from me something I heard from the Messenger of Allah: A man is not asked about why he beats his wife!'

The speech is clear. This means, through the narration, that Umar's wife was not sleeping next to him... **This means that in the middle of the night, he woke up from his sleep, remembered something, and went to his wife to beat her!**

This exact same hadith is present in numerous sources. Among the sources in which this hadith is mentioned is **Sunan Abi Dawud**... Abu Dawud seems to have chewed it up from the very beginning! He just entered the hadith directly and said: 'A man is not asked about why he beats his wife.' The rest of the hadith is missing, only this part is mentioned. The entire story is omitted!

There is a strange incident that came in **Tabaqat Ibn Sa'd**... regarding Umar's marriage to 'Atikah bint Zayd. She devoted herself and cut herself off from men. And men began proposing to her, and she kept refusing.
So Umar said to her guardian: 'Mention me to her.' So he mentioned him to her, and she refused Umar as well! So Umar said: 'Marry her to me!'
First, she is a devoted woman... Second, she refused! ... So he married her to him! Meaning it was not she who married herself [consented].
So Umar came to her, entered upon her, and wrestled with her ('Arakaha) until he overpowered her over herself, and consummated the marriage (Nakahaha)!
This is from Tabaqat Ibn Sa'd, from the eighth volume! And the incidents are many, very many. **What do you say to the likes of this speech?!**
Segment 12: The Assault on the Mourners - Beating Aisha and Umm Farwah

Sheikh al-Ghizzi unveils a series of shocking historical accounts from Kanz al-'Ummal and Tarikh al-Tabari detailing Umar's violent suppression of the women mourning the death of Abu Bakr. Despite Aisha being the "Mother of the Believers" and the daughter of the deceased Caliph, Umar repeatedly ordered his enforcer, Hisham ibn al-Walid, to raid her home. Disregarding Aisha's explicit religious protests against entering her home without permission, Umar commanded his men to drag the women out one by one into the street. The texts explicitly state that Umar then physically beat Aisha, Abu Bakr's sister Umm Farwah, and the other mourning women with his infamous whip (Durrah) until they scattered in terror.
I said, and this is **Kanz al-'Ummal**, this is the fifteenth and sixteenth volume... From Sa'id ibn al-Musayyab, he said:

When Abu Bakr died, Aisha established mourning for him. It reached Umar, so he forbade her from mourning over Abu Bakr. But they refused to stop. So he said to Hisham ibn al-Walid: 'Go out to the daughter of Abu Quhafah [Aisha]!' So he struck her repeatedly with the whip ('Alaha bil-Durrati Darabat)! So the [other] mourning women scattered when they heard that!
**If Aisha was beaten, then what about the rest of the mourning women who were weeping over Abu Bakr?**
A second narration... 'And it is authentic (Sahih).' ... So Umar said to Hisham ibn al-Walid: 'Stand and bring out the women!'
So Aisha said to Hisham ibn al-Walid: 'I strictly forbid you (Uharrijuk)!' ... So Umar said: 'Enter, for I have given you permission!'
He enters houses without permission. Aisha said: 'I strictly forbid you.' Umar said: 'Enter, for I have given you permission.' Even if she forbids you, you must enter!
So Hisham ibn al-Walid entered upon the women... So he began bringing them out, woman by woman, and he [Umar] was beating them with the whip (Yadribuhunna bil-Durrah)!

Meaning Hisham ibn al-Walid brought the women out one by one, and Umar was striking them in a line! 'He began bringing them out, woman by woman, and he was beating them with the whip, until Umm Farwah came out.' Umm Farwah is the sister of Abu Bakr... This is the sister of the Caliph of the Muslims! And he separated between them.
Let us go to Tarikh al-Tabari... 'So Umar said to Hisham: Enter, I have given you permission!'
So Hisham entered, and brought Umm Farwah, the sister of Abu Bakr, out to Umar. So he struck her repeatedly with the whip! So the mourning women scattered when they heard that.
You notice the multiple incursions into the house of Aisha! ... These are the morals of the Caliph. And these are the behaviors he used to act upon.
Segment 13: The Assault on the Mourners of Khalid - "She Has No Sanctity!"

Sheikh al-Ghizzi brings forward further evidence from Kanz al-'Ummal demonstrating Umar's relentless physical abuse of women. When women gathered to mourn the death of Khalid ibn al-Walid, Umar ordered them dragged out and beat them with his whip. When one woman's veil fell off, exposing her hair, onlookers pleaded with him to stop, but Umar coldly replied, "Leave her, for she has no sanctity!" The Sheikh draws a harrowing parallel: if the Muslims of that time were shocked that Umar felt no hesitation in stripping the modesty of ordinary Muslim women, why did they not marvel at his violation of the supreme sanctity of Lady Fatima?
And the limit does not stop at this matter. Let us go to page 308 of Kanz al-'Ummal, the fifteenth volume... From 'Amr ibn Dinar, he said:

When Khalid ibn al-Walid died, women gathered in the house of Maymunah weeping. So Umar came, and with him was Ibn Abbas, and he had the whip (Durrah)... So he began bringing them out to him, and he was beating them with the whip! So the veil (Khimar) of a woman among them fell off! They said: 'O Commander of the Believers, her veil!' So he said: 'Leave her, for she has no sanctity (La Hurmata laha)!'

The people, the Muslims, were amazed by his saying 'she has no sanctity'. They marveled at this! This is the state of the people... **So why did the people not marvel at the assault on the sanctity of the daughter of the Messenger of Allah?!**
Sulaym said: 'I said: O Salman! Did they enter, and there was no seeking of permission?'

He said: 'Yes, by the glory of the Compeller! While al-Zahra had no veil on! But she sought refuge behind the door out of care for covering and hijab. But when they saw her, they squeezed her a squeeze, that she almost died from it!'
In page 309 of the same volume... From Nasr ibn Abi Asim:

Umar heard a mourning woman in Medina at night, so he came to her, entered upon her without seeking permission, and dispersed the women! He caught the mourning woman and began beating her with the whip, so her veil fell off! They said: 'Her hair, O Commander of the Believers!' He said: 'Yes, she has no sanctity!'
**This is Umar! He does not preserve the sanctity of anyone!** He does not preserve the sanctity of these women, he enters houses without seeking permission, with this harshness!
Segment 14: The Final Verdict and the Elegies of the Scholars

In the final segment, Sheikh al-Ghizzi brings his epistemological argument full circle: having exhaustively documented Umar's lifelong pattern of brutalizing women and invading homes, the Muslim who "associates" with this history can no longer be surprised by the assault on Lady Fatima's house. It was not an anomaly; it was his established nature. The Sheikh then recites profound poetry from two great Shia scholars, Sheikh Muhammad Hussein al-Isfahani and Sheikh Salih al-Kawwaz. These elegies draw a direct, theological, and historical line from the burning door and the broken rib of Fatima in Medina to the crushed chest and severed hands of Imam Hussein and his companions in Karbala. The Sheikh concludes the episode by directly addressing the Shia today, condemning those who deny Fatima's oppression, and praying for the awaited revenge by the Imam of the Time.

What do we deduce about this personality? We are now like the one who has associated ('Ashara) with this personality! ... I say: What do the fair-minded deduce? The people of intellect, and the people of sound logic, from all these facts?

**The one who associates with a human being, and sees all these descriptions in him, when it is said to him that a house was attacked, and striking and killing occurred, and what happened happened—he does not find that strange!** This is a natural matter because these are from his traits and his nature.
Time has caught up with us... Therefore, this is the Diwan of Al-Anwar al-Qudsiyyah by our Sheikh Muhammad Hussein al-Isfahani... What does our Sheikh al-Isfahani say?

My grief for her! Her status was lost, Until her full moon was hidden by the veil! ... Indeed, the tale of the door is full of sorrows, From what the assault of the enemies brought about... What did they gain with the fire other than shame? And behind it is the torment of the Fire! ... And from the springing of blood from her breasts, The greatness of what happened to her is known. And he exceeded the limit by slapping the cheek! May the hand of tyranny and transgression be paralyzed! ... And for the whips, there is a ringing echo, In the ear of time, how sorrowful it is!
You hear the echo of the whips with which Fatima was struck! The echo still reverberates in every day. She is struck by these whips in our time; she is struck by the whip of tongues!
The hardest thing upon the oppressed is that their oppression is denied! And the hardest thing upon the oppressed is that their oppressor is exonerated!
I address you, O Shia of the Ahlulbayt in Iraq! ... **The hardest thing upon the oppressed is that their oppression is denied, and that their oppressor is exonerated!**
The poem continues:

And the remaining trace is like a bracelet, On the arm of al-Zahra, the strongest of proofs... And the thrusting of the sword's scabbard into her sides, Brought about all that came upon her... The criminal committed a crime against her embryo, So the mountains crumbled from her wailing! ... Is the distressed, wounded one prevented, From weeping, out of fear of scandal?!
And the same thing is repeating now. Those who defend the oppressor of Fatima... it is to preserve political positions, to achieve political interests, personal interests!

By Allah, it is fitting for her to weep blood, As long as the earth remains and the sky turns! ... She was not buried at night secretly, Except due to her anger against the people of cruelty! Woe unto them from the wrath of the Compeller, For their oppression of the flower of the Chosen One!
I conclude my speech with verses from a poem by Sheikh Salih al-Kawwaz... From the sweetest of Shia poetry! ... What does he say?

With the breaking of the one who was pure in Karbala, A chest that clamored with blood, and a forehead! Had it not been for the fall of the fetus of Fatima, No fetus would have been killed for her in Karbala! And with the breaking of that rib, a chest was crushed, In which the secret of the Divine is pictured! ... He rebuked her with the whip... And they were adorned in Al-Taff [Karbala] by the rebuking with blackened backs! And by their cutting of that Arak tree, without her reaching it, A hand was cut in Karbala and severed!

He says: 'My lady, O daughter of the Messenger of Allah, this is our effort. My effort and the effort of my brothers, and it is the effort of the incapable. An apology to you, O daughter of Muhammad, prayers of Allah be upon you.'

**May Allah cool your chest with the appearance of your son, Al-Hujjah ibn al-Hassan, prayers of Allah and His peace be upon him.** Our eyes, our hearts, our minds are on the path waiting... I ask you all for supplication. And tomorrow I will meet you in the final episode of the Fatimi File. In the safety of Allah.

title: The File of Fatima - Episode 7: The Sacrifice of Fatima
speaker: Sheikh Abdul-Halim al-Ghizzi

content.text: Overview: Episode 7

In the final episode of the File of Fatima, Sheikh al-Ghizzi explores the deep theological and cosmic reasons behind Imam Ali's patience during the assault on his home. Through the lens of the philosophy of history, the Sheikh explains that the Prophet's **cosmic program** for humanity required a profound sacrifice to safeguard its future. Just as Prophets Lot and Abraham made agonizing sacrifices to preserve divine values, Imam Ali had to present Lady Fatima as the **concentrated sacrifice** for the Imamate. This supreme sacrifice was later mirrored and physically explained on the plains of Karbala by Imam Hussein. The episode concludes with **harrowing final historical snapshots** of the burning door and the stolen deed of Fadak, culminating in a deeply emotional elegy that forever binds the tragedy of Medina to the tragedy of Karbala.
Segment 1: Introduction and the Ziyarat of Imam al-Askari

Sheikh al-Ghizzi opens the seventh and final episode of the File of Fatima with a profound recitation of the Ziyarat taught by Imam Hasan al-Askari. This specific prayer calls upon Allah to take revenge on those who oppressed Lady Fatima, usurped her rights, and shed the tears of her children. The Sheikh outlines the structure of this final episode: first, analyzing the true meaning of the Prophet's command for Imam Ali to "be patient" (the Wasiyyah); second, adding final painful historical snapshots to the Fatimi File; and finally, concluding with an emotional elegy comparing the tragedies of Medina and Karbala.

The Prophet said: 'May your father be your sacrifice, O Zahra, O Zahra!' 'Indeed, the essence of creation belongs to you, O Zahra, O Zahra!' 'O you in whom my Lord is pleased, O wife of Ali in her highness, O Zahra, O Zahra!'

Fatima, and Al-Mustafa her father, and Al-Murtada her husband, and the Infallibles from her children and her sons. Peace be upon the Fatimids in every time and place, from her offspring, her Shia, and her lovers, and the mercy of Allah and His blessings.

The Fatimi File. The seventh episode, and the final one at the same time, from the episodes of this program. The first thing I open my speech with is the speech of our Imam Al-Hasan the Pure, Al-Askari, prayers of Allah and His peace be upon him. There are very important prayers (Salawat) recommended to be recited, mentioned by the traditionist Al-Qummi in Mafatih al-Jinan.

O Allah, send prayers upon the Truthful Lady, Fatima the Pure... O Allah, be the seeker of revenge for her against those who oppressed her and belittled her right! O Allah, be the seeker of revenge for her against those who oppressed her and belittled her right! And be the avenger, O Allah, for the tears of her children!

My speech in this episode will fall into several directions. The first direction will be a discussion about the Will (Wasiyyah) which is repeated and frequently mentioned in the narrations of the Ahlulbayt: the Will of the Prophet to Ali regarding patience and suppressing anger!

And the second direction: Pictures, snapshots to be added to the file. And after all that, I will go to a conclusion to seal this file with. Even though this file cannot be sealed! **The file is open; this file will not be sealed except by the Imam of our Time, prayers of Allah and His peace be upon him.**
Segment 2: The Philosophy of History and the Prophet's Cosmic Program

Sheikh al-Ghizzi addresses the central question of the episode: Why was Imam Ali commanded to be patient? To answer this, he introduces a framework based on the philosophy of history. He explains that while ordinary events require only surface-level analysis, extraordinary personalities and paradigm-shifting events require deep, systemic understanding. He reveals that the Prophet did not merely bring a set of daily rituals; he brought a cosmic "Program" consisting of two parts: Hidayah (present guidance) and the Future (the ultimate realization of humanity's divine vicegerency on Earth). The Sheikh explains that humanity is currently in a state of embryonic gestation, and this world is merely a womb. The "Will" (Wasiyyah) was not a mundane request to avoid a fight; it was a strict directive for Imam Ali—the ultimate Trustee of this Program—to safeguard the long-term future of human integration at all costs.

The first direction that will be discussed is the Will (Wasiyyah). "Ali was tied and bound by the ropes of the Will." What is this Will? The Will, in its totality and in its reality, is patience! This is the general meaning and the general title for this Will.

There are ordinary matters that we can call the daily routine in life, and there are matters that are not ordinary; they have a specificity... Extraordinary matters are the matters that deserve analysis because givens surround them: before them, inside them, and after them. When the speech is about a Will from the Greatest Prophet to the Master of Legatees, regarding a tremendously dangerous issue, which is the issue of the Imamate, upon which Guidance (Hidayah) depends on one hand, and the Future depends on the other...

The Prophet brought a program comprising two dimensions: **The first dimension is Guidance (Hidayah)** for the people according to every time, every place, and every intellect. **And the second section, or the higher section of this program, which is the Future of Humanity!**

Humanity is on its way to what? To a massive transformation! This massive transformation will lead them to the Day of Resurrection. **Humanity right now is in a state of growth and integration, just like a fetus in the mother's womb! This world was only expressed as being a "prison" because it is a womb!**

In a precise phrase: **It is the program of the Caliphate (Vicegerency) on Earth!** Because the Caliphate on Earth has not been realized in its most perfect form, and it cannot be realized except by the realization of the future Muhammadan program! This is our doctrine which we believe in.

In this program, trustees must stand over it. And the first of these trustees is Ali, prayers of Allah and His peace be upon him! Ali is the trustee of this program. A trustee over the program of Guidance... A trustee over the secrets of this humanity, and a trustee over the divine secrets.
**The Will is exactly this! The Will is the trust of Muhammad, blessings of Allah be upon him and his family, in preserving his divine program in its two branches: Guidance and the Future.**
Segment 3: The Philosophy of Creation and the Interconnection of Cosmos and Legislation

Sheikh al-Ghizzi delves into a profound theological and philosophical explanation of why humanity was created. Using the beautiful analogy of a bottle of perfume, he explains that Divine Power, by its very infinite nature, necessitates manifestation. This manifestation required a creature capable of reflecting all cosmic probabilities. Drawing upon verses from the Quran, the Sheikh breaks down the dual nature of the human being: the lowly, material "clay" and the exalted, divine "breath" (Ruh). He concludes this section by establishing a critical rule for understanding the Great Crime: Divine Legislation (Tashri') is not merely a set of social rules, but the direct, physical shadow of Cosmic Reality (Takwin). He points out that when humanity obeys or disobeys divine law, the physical universe itself reacts, citing the promise to the People of the Book and the flood that destroyed the people of Saba.

Why were we created? How was this creation found? This creation was found for a deep reason, which is that **the Divine Power itself necessitates that it manifests!** When you open a bottle of perfume, the perfume spreads in all corners of the place... The perfume spread because the perfume, from its own essence, from its nature, if it appears, its scent spreads!

The Divine Power, the Power that has no limits, it necessitates from its essence and by its essence to manifest! ... So among the manifestations of Power is that there is a creature... that this creature gathers all the probabilities present in the world in which he will live.
**So this human was created from two aspects: from a material, earthy aspect, and from a lordly, divine aspect!**
And when I have proportioned him and breathed into him of My soul, then fall down to him in prostration.

There is the clay from an altered black mud, which is the earthy aspect. And there is a second aspect, which is a lordly aspect. **Hearing, vision, and hearts are the manifestations of the lordly breath! Had it not been for this breath in that clay, the human would have remained clay, a stone!** The essential aspect, the core of the human, is in this breath!

Allah, the Glorified and Exalted, made this human free. The legislative (Tashri'i) aspect is a legal, organizational aspect. Humans, when they write a system, it is a disconnected system having no relation to the physical dimension of people's lives. **As for the Divine Legislation (Tashri'), it is connected with the physical dimension of these creatures!**

And this is what can be termed as the embrace and blending between the Cosmos (Takwin) and Legislation (Tashri'). **Legislation is the shadow of the Cosmos!** This Cosmos, its shadows reflect in the Legislation, in the laws.
For example, when we read in the sixty-sixth verse of Surah Al-Ma'idah:
And if only they had upheld the Torah and the Gospel, and what was revealed to them from their Lord, they would have consumed [provision] from above them and from beneath their feet.
If legislations had no connection to the Cosmos, then what is the meaning of this?! Because Legislation has a connection to the Cosmos.
When we go to Surah Ar-Ra'd. The Surah that speaks about a law many of you have memorized:
Indeed, Allah will not change the condition of a people until they change what is in themselves.

Even political change, social change, and the change in the issue of guidance and misguidance is an issue connected to the Cosmos! ... In Surah Saba: "So We sent upon them the flood of the dam..." There is a change in the Cosmos. **This recompense is the interconnection between the Cosmos and Legislation!**
Segment 4: The Cosmic Anchors and the Concept of Sacrifice (Qurban)

Sheikh al-Ghizzi provides examples from Shia hadith demonstrating that the Ahlulbayt and their true followers literally sustain the physical universe. Quoting Rijal al-Kashshi, he shows that the earth is provided for and protected by a select few—specifically naming the seven Companions who secretly prayed over Lady Fatima's body. He connects the concept of protecting divine values with the agonizing sacrifices made by the Prophets. He uses the Quranic stories of Prophet Lot offering his daughters to protect his guests, and Prophet Abraham abandoning his beloved wife Hagar and son Ishmael in the barren desert of Mecca. The Sheikh highlights that Abraham’s ultimate test—the command to physically slaughter Ishmael—was the sacrifice required to establish the covenant of Imamate. He emphasizes that preserving divine principles often demands unthinkable personal sacrifices, setting the stage to explain why Imam Ali had to accept the sacrifice of Lady Fatima.
Also, in our narrations, there are clear indications regarding this human creature. For example, this is **Rijal al-Kashshi**. He said:

The earth was constricted to seven [people]... through them you are provided for, and through them you are given victory! ... And Ali, peace be upon him, used to say: 'And I am their Imam! And they are the ones who prayed over Fatima!'

These are Alawis! Fatimids! "And they are the ones who prayed over Fatima!" From those whom Fatima permitted to pray over her. There is a cosmic and legislative effect to this human creature. This trust in the legislative aspect is what generated this cosmic aspect.

So when we deal with Ali, we must deal with Ali with this understanding! With this depth! ... The Program was in need of a sacrifice (Qurban)! And here you say: What does that mean? I say, let us go to the Quran.
When we read in Surah Hud, verse seventy-eight, in the story of Lot, peace be upon him:
He said, 'O my people, these are my daughters; they are purer for you. So fear Allah and do not disgrace me [concerning my guests].'

Is this not a sacrifice (Qurban) he is presenting here? In defense of what? In defense of a value. **Sometimes principles and values require sacrifices!** So if Lot wanted to preserve this value... he presented this sacrifice.

And likewise, when we go to the story of Abraham. When he took Hagar and her son Ishmael, and left them in the desert, in the desert of Mecca... He left his wife whom he loves, and who bore him Ishmael, a pearl from the pearls of Paradise!
So Abraham said: 'The One who commanded me to place you in this location is the One who will suffice you.'

He left her in that desert with his child. This is a sacrifice! And after this sacrifice comes the story of the slaughter! And how he took his son Ishmael... and wanted to slaughter him as a sheep is slaughtered! And Ishmael submitted. **And from here began the contract of Imamate!**

These rituals of Hajj... are tied to the core of Imamate! **Abraham presented a sacrifice (Qurban) for the Imamate! The sacrifice of Imamate was to slaughter Ishmael!** But Allah, the Glorified and Exalted, ransomed him with a great sacrifice. This is regarding what passed. As for what came after Fatima, it is Karbala.
Segment 5: The Concentrated Text and Its Bloody Explanation

Sheikh al-Ghizzi connects the sacrifice of Lady Fatima to the Tragedy of Karbala, explaining that the two events are fundamentally identical in purpose. He states that Lady Fatima was the "Concentrated Text" (Matn Murakkaz)—a pure, intense sacrifice designed to preserve the Prophet's cosmic program. Because concentrated texts are difficult to understand, they require extensive commentary. Karbala was that commentary. Imam Hussein bringing his family to the desert, allowing his infant to be slaughtered, and watching his women be taken captive was the exact same philosophical and theological act as Imam Ali accepting the assault on his pregnant wife. Both were necessary sacrifices (Qarabin) to generate the overwhelming emotional energy required to build the global, trans-historical base of Shia followers who will pave the way for Imam al-Mahdi.

As for what came after Fatima, it is Karbala. Karbala is what explains and clarifies for us what happened to Fatima! When Al-Hussein, peace be upon him, carried his family, and when he began to mobilize the armies of emotions...

Across this history, what achieved victory? It is the army of emotions! **These emotions are what changed convictions and created the base paving the way for the Muhammadan program for the future!** How was the paving base born? The paving base, the Shia of the Ahlulbayt, this is the paving base for the program of the future in whatever land they are!
**Fatima is the concentrated text! The concentrated sacrifice (Qurban). And its explanation came in Karbala!** Beautiful verses by the judge Ibn Qari'ah... He says:

And I would have shown you that Al-Hussein was struck from the Day of the Saqifah! And for what reason was Fatima the honorable buried at night? ... Ah for the daughter of Muhammad, she died choking on her grief!

And this meaning is present and clear in the words of the Ahlulbayt. The Ahlulbayt say Al-Hussein was killed from the Day of the Saqifah. In fact, why from the Day of the Saqifah? Because the Saqifah is for the Sahifah (the written document)!

Fatima, as I said a short while ago, is the concentrated, condensed text. She was a sacrifice for what? A sacrifice to preserve this program! ... When the Lady of the Ladies [Zaynab] advanced on the night of the eleventh... she placed her hands under his back and raised it towards the sky and said:
O Allah, accept from the Family of Muhammad this sacrifice!
This sacrifice was an explanation for the concentrated sacrifice of Fatima! When the rib was broken, the ribs were crushed completely in the Taff [Karbala]!

We, when we read Karbala, and when we read Al-Hussein as he brings his infant while knowing he will be slaughtered... All of this for what? **To mobilize emotions! To create a generator; this generator generates heat, it gives energy.** This great generator took its energy from a concentrated generator! From Fatima!

The exact same philosophy by which Al-Hussein came with his family and presented his family as food, and presented his family as a victim in the hands of those beasts... is the exact same philosophy by which Ali acted! ... **He willed to see Fatima squeezed between the door and the wall! And the nail piercing the chest of the daughter of Muhammad! And her fetus killed!**

But the darkness and the pain was with Ali! Because what happened to the daughters of Fatima was after Al-Hussein had departed... but all of this happened within sight and hearing of Ali! ... But as he said to her, he said: **"Indeed, that is small in the presence of Allah!"** This pain that has no limits, indeed it is small in the presence of Allah! This is Ali! And this is the meaning of the Will (Wasiyyah).

If Zaynab, on the day of Taff, placed her hands under the back of Al-Hussein and raised him to the sky and said: "O Allah, accept from the Family of Muhammad this sacrifice!" **Fatima was presented by Ali!** ... The Husseini sacrifice was presented by Zaynab; the Fatimi sacrifice was presented by Ali!
Segment 6: The Final Snapshots - The Torn Document, the Burning Door, and the Heavenly Farewell

Sheikh al-Ghizzi presents the second direction of the episode: a final collection of agonizing "snapshots" to be permanently added to the Fatimi File. He begins with a modern poetic elegy lamenting how the physical assault on Lady Fatima in the 11th year of Hijrah is mirrored today by the intellectual assault of those who deny her tragedy using "dynamic and modern" methods, asking them one devastating question: If nothing happened, why is her grave still lost? He then cites historical texts detailing Umar violently kicking her to steal the deed of Fadak, her own harrowing description of the burning door and her miscarriage, and the heartbreaking final moments of her life when Imam Ali pleaded with her to open her eyes. The segment concludes with the celestial tragedy of Imam Hasan and Imam Hussein weeping upon her shrouded chest until a voice from the heavens begged Imam Ali to separate them because their grief was making the angels weep.

A collection of pictures. The first picture: lines from a whispered prayer (Munajat) with the daughter of the Messenger of Allah... I will read lines from it. This is the first picture from the pictures I add to the pages of this file.

O Zahra... If they killed you through torture and exemplary punishment, my Lady, in the eleventh year after the Hijrah... O Zahra, they killed you today with a thousand and one tongues! ... They oppose her today with the dynamic, active, open, and errant method: 'Indeed, the hadith of the door, the nail, and the attack of the people on the house is not a reliable hadith!' ... But a question confuses me... it digs into the depths of the conscience: Why was the grave lost to this day? And after this day, O human?

Another picture from the book of Sayyid al-Muqarram, The Life of the Great Truthful Lady... Sayyid al-Muqarram paints this picture for us: "So she exited from his presence... and the document was with her. So Umar encountered her on the way... So he demanded the document from her. Umar demanded the document from Fatima! But she refused."
So he kicked her with his foot, and took the document from her by force, spat on it, and tore it up! ... So she, peace be upon her, said: 'You tore my document, may Allah tear your belly!'
And another picture that passed upon us. This picture: Fatima says in her Will:

So they gathered thick firewood at our door... and brought fire to burn it, and burn us! ... So I stood behind the door and pleaded with them by Allah and by my father to leave us alone and support us. So Umar took the whip from the hand of Qunfudh... and he kicked the door with his foot! So I fell on my face while the fire was blazing and scorching my face! He struck me with his hand until my earring scattered from my ear! And the labor pains came to me, so I miscarried Muhsin, murdered without a crime!

And this is another picture we add to the collection of pictures of the Fatimi File. This is **Bihar al-Anwar**, the forty-third volume. "And the Commander of the Believers, peace be upon him... approached wanting the house. Suddenly, maidens met him... weeping and sorrowful! So he said to them: 'What is the news?' ... So we said: 'O Commander of the Believers! Catch your cousin Zahra, and we do not think you will catch her [alive]!'"

He entered upon Fatima, and behold, she was cast upon her bed... and she was grasping right and stretching left! ... And he called out to her: 'O Zahra!' But she did not speak to him... He called out to her: 'O Fatima! Speak to me, I am your cousin Ali ibn Abi Talib!' ... So she opened her eyes in his face, looked at him, and wept, and he wept!

The final picture I add, then I go to the conclusion of the program. The final picture transmitted by Sayyid al-Muqarram... "When the Commander of the Believers shrouded her, before tying the knot of the shroud... He called out: 'O Hasan! O Hussein! O Zaynab! Come and take your farewell from your mother!'"

So Al-Hasan and Al-Hussein came and fell upon their mother... 'If you meet our grandfather, O Zahra... tell him: We remained orphans after you in the abode of the world!' So the Commander of the Believers, peace be upon him, said: 'I testify to Allah that she moaned, wailed, extended her hands, and hugged them to her chest! Suddenly, a caller from the sky called out: O Abu al-Hasan! Lift them from her, for by Allah they have made the angels of the heavens weep!'

And it was the final farewell. Farewell, O Fatima. O daughter of the Messenger of Allah. And this is the final picture I add to the file of the daughter of Muhammad, blessings of Allah be upon him and his family.
Segment 7: The Final Elegy - The Wounds of the Saqifah and the Tears of Karbala

Sheikh al-Ghizzi concludes the File of Fatima with a deeply emotional, poetic elegy of his own composition. He bridges the theological and historical gap between the crushing of Lady Fatima behind the door in Medina and the crushing of Imam Hussein's chest under the hooves of horses in Karbala. He draws a heartbreaking parallel between the slaughtered infant Abdullah (Ali al-Asghar) and the miscarried infant Muhsin. The Sheikh formally closes the file, dedicating his lifelong efforts entirely to Lady Fatima, asking only for her intercession for his team and the Fatimid viewers, and promising a new investigative file in the near future.

What remains with me is the conclusion of the program. It is lines I wrote for this program. Lines, I do not know what they are. Are they poetry, or prose? Or are they a yearning, or the speech of the conscience? By Allah, I do not know what they are.

To the one, O Hussein, who did not miscarry the likes of her... Carrying like your carrying, and did not nurse... I wrote on the shores of wounds a burning sigh. I reckon it exited from the heart and I imagine it echoing in the folds of the pericardium.

Teach me to find an excuse for those who betrayed Al-Ghadir and abandoned their firm covenants! The 'Sahifah' was written, O woe unto them for what their pens put forward. The fire is blazing since that day, and the hearts are the hearts. Ah, what do I say? Do I uncover my wound, when the wounds are bleeding?

The 'Sahifah' was written, and they rushed, pushing each other in groups towards the Saqifah, as if its courtyard was the courtyard of Al-Taff [Karbala]. By Allah, I know no calm; the fire is exploding at the honorable door of Fatima! And its blaze is in Al-Ghadhariyyat [Karbala], the neighing of horses, and foul tongues like the stumble of Umayya's caliph.

O you who filled the Shari'ah with a tragedy... Where Hussein is on the earth, the horses of the enemies crushed his ribs, killed by the family of Umayya, thirsty beside the Shari'ah, and his infant with a severed jugular vein, covered in blood, so seek the infant.

Muhsin is this, speaking to the infant saying: 'Abdullah, hurry!' 'For a people hastened your slaughter without water; they hastened my slaughter before I could breathe anything of their air!' ... And Fatima, my mother... a crushed chest, and broken ribs, and the eye of Muhammad in her eye...

And a sword! For God's sake, this one! And a waist that was pierced by kicking! And what a sword, for God's sake! O Zahra! And the pains of labor, and the squeezing, and fire at the doors, and disbelief in all virtue and destiny. And above this, the daughter of Muhammad is missing her grave!

Abdullah, my mother, her milk did not dry up; rather, her blood flowed! And whoever sucked blood from her breasts knows the magnitude of what happened to her! And I do not know the news of the nail, ask her chest, the treasury of secrets! ... Abdullah, my mother, they prevented her from weeping! She was expelled from her house, her Arak tree under which she shaded herself was cut down, and she retreated to the House of Sorrows, lonely, a stranger, pouring tears and drinking agony... May Allah not elevate after the House of Sorrows any house of joy!
O Allah, send prayers upon Fatima, her father, her husband, her sons, and the secret entrusted within her.

My Lady, O daughter of the Messenger of Allah, this is the final paper I add to the Fatimi File. To the file I was honored to name with your pure, purified name. I do not seek anything for myself; it is enough honor, dignity, and glory for me to be counted among your slaves and servants.

If I want something, I ask that for my brothers who helped me and assisted me in serving Muhammad and the family of Muhammad... I ask Allah, the Glorified and Exalted, to grant them success, and their families, and all the Fatimid men and Fatimid women, from our brothers and our lovers, from those who loved Fatima and aligned with her guardians, and disassociated from her enemies.

And as for you, O Fatimid men and Fatimid women, perhaps loneliness will strike you with the interruption of this program. It is not a loneliness for my speech—for what is my value?—it is a loneliness for the speech about Fatima, for the speech of Muhammad and the family of Muhammad. I promise you very soon, I will open a new file, with many truths and many topics. A new file, a new topic, soon, God willing the Exalted.
I ask you all for supplication. In the safety of Allah.

title: The File of Ali - Episode 1: The Will
speaker: Sheikh Abdul-Halim al-Ghizzi

content.text: Overview: Episode 1

In this inaugural episode of the new series, Sheikh al-Ghizzi explores the foundational concept of the **Will (Wasiyya)**. Moving away from technical grading of transmission chains, he argues that leaving a will is a self-evident human instinct (**Fitra**), making it irrational to claim the Prophet Muhammad (SAWA) abandoned his Ummah without clear leadership. The episode delves into primary historical documents, primarily from Sunni sources like **Tarikh al-Tabari**, to expose the hypocrisy of the early Caliphs who actively secured their own successions while denying the Prophet's right to do the same. The Sheikh also unveils the "Documentary Theft" used by later historians to censor Imam Ali's clear, early appointment as the **Wasi** (Executor) and **Khalifa** (Successor).
Segment 1: The Instinct of the Will (Fitra)

Sheikh al-Ghizzi opens with a spiritual salutation to the "Face of Allah" and the followers of "Abu Turab" (Ali). He declares that this new series will not be a technical exercise in grading chains of narration but an exploration of self-evident truths found in history and the human soul. He argues that the concept of a Will (Wasiyya) is a natural instinct (Fitra); just as a parent or a child instinctively provides instructions before leaving, it is irrational to believe the Prophet would leave the core of Islam to chance. He then introduces historical evidence from Tarikh al-Tabari showing that even those who denied the Prophet's Will felt the need to write their own wills to secure power.

In the name of Allah, the Most Gracious, the Most Merciful. Peace be upon the Radiant **Face of Allah** and His High Side. Peace be upon the ripe fruit and the brilliant star. Peace be upon **Abu al-Hassan Ali**, and the mercy of Allah and His blessings. Peace be upon you, O **Shi'a of Abu Turab**, in every corner of this existence—living and dead, young and old, men and women.

**The File of Ali - Episode 1.** The beginning is from the **Fitra** (innate nature) and the conscience (al-Wujdan). As was mentioned in "The File of Fatima," I will not play the game of "chains and sources"—this frail, spider-web-like game. The Truth is clear and explicit, and the logic of reality points to itself. I will browse pages from our history that still contain many clues despite all the distortion and can be found here and there.

The **Will** is a matter judged by the **Fitra** and the conscience, regardless of what is said or what weak excuses or "interpretations" are brought. Traditionists, theologians, and historians have spoken at length—sometimes truthfully, sometimes distorting or hiding facts. But the Fitra, the conscience, and eventually the **Reason** remain the clear evidence and the shining proof that accepts no debate. Yes, stubbornness can come and cover the evidence of the conscience, but it is a covering that is not real; inside the person, the matter is clear and evident.

No sane person who has the slightest level of concern for any affair in their life—if they know they will not continue to oversee it due to illness, travel, or death—would fail to leave a will. The conscience, the Fitra, and the Reason all command the necessity of a will here. A person is driven to make a will in such circumstances by an innate motive without even thinking.

This innate drive is present even in children. If a child leaves their toys scattered and has to go out, they will tell someone in the house to look after their toys. Even if they travel, they will call back to ask about their needs and things they care about. This is the logic of the human soul. Is it then conceivable that the Master of all human souls—indeed, the Master of all beings, **Muhammad** (peace be upon him and his family)—would depart from this natural system? Would he leave the most important matter of his life—Islam, faith, the Ummah, guidance, and misguidance—to be played with by whims and ignorant souls? It is impossible.

When we browse history, what do we find? We find it in the very history of those who denied the Will and said that Muhammad did not appoint anyone. For example, let us go to **Tarikh al-Tabari** (Volume 2, page 591), regarding the final days of the first Caliph, Abu Bakr, during his terminal illness. He called Uthman into a private meeting and said:
Write: In the name of Allah, the Most Gracious, the Most Merciful. This is what Abu Bakr ibn Abi Quhafa has covenanted to the Muslims...
He felt the natural, innate need to secure the succession, yet they claim the Messenger of Allah did not do the same for his Ummah.
Segment 2: The Double Standard of Succession

Sheikh al-Ghizzi continues to analyze the historical records of the early successions to demonstrate the hypocrisy of denying the Prophet’s Will. He focuses on the appointment of Umar by Abu Bakr, noting that Abu Bakr did not leave the matter to "consultation" (Shura) but made a definitive, written appointment while he was drifting in and out of consciousness. The Sheikh then highlights the irony: while the Prophet's request for a pen and paper on his deathbed was blocked by Umar with the claim that "pain has overcome him," the same group accepted Abu Bakr’s written decree under similar medical circumstances. He describes the atmosphere of Umar’s succession as one of intimidation.

We find that the same people who claimed the Prophet left the Ummah without a successor were the most eager to appoint their own successors. Look at the text in **Tarikh al-Tabari**. It says that Abu Bakr fainted while dictating the document. Uthman, seeing him unconscious, feared he would die without finishing, so Uthman wrote Umar’s name on his own initiative.
When Abu Bakr woke up, he asked: "What did you write?" Uthman told him he had written Umar’s name. Abu Bakr replied:
May Allah reward you with good, for if you had written your own name, you would have been worthy of it.

Where was the **Shura** (consultation) here? Where was the "consensus of the companions"? It was a private appointment. But the point I want to emphasize for your conscience is the double standard. When the Messenger of Allah, who is "protected from error," asked for a pen to write a document that would prevent misguidance, Umar stopped him and said:
The man is delirious.

Yet, when Abu Bakr was literally fainting and losing consciousness—actually delirious from the agony of death—they accepted his document as the "Covenant of the Caliphate." This is the **Reality of the World**. They didn't reject the concept of a Will; they only rejected the **Prophet's Will** because it named Ali.

Then we move to the Will of Umar. When he was dying, he created a committee of six men. But look at the language used—it was a will full of blood and threats. He gave instructions that if the committee did not agree within three days, the minority should be beheaded. If they were split three-against-three, the side that didn't have **Abd al-Rahman ibn Awf** should be killed.

Is this the "Religion of Mercy"? A succession plan based on the threat of the sword against the elite companions of the Prophet? This demonstrates that even the leaders of Saqifa knew that without a "Will," the state would collapse. They followed their Fitra to protect their worldly power, but they fought against the Fitra when it came to the **Divine Right** of the Household of Muhammad.

So, the question is not "Did the Prophet leave a Will?" The question is **"Why did they suppress it?"** History proves that the principle of succession was practiced by everyone—except, according to their claim, the one who was the most responsible for the Ummah's future. This logic is shattered by the historical documents found in their own books.
Segment 3: The Universal Sunnah and The Day of Warning

Sheikh al-Ghizzi argues that the appointment of a successor is a Universal Sunnah (practice) of all Prophets since Adam. He cites the principle that "every Prophet has a Wasi (executor/successor)" and asserts that Muhammad (PBUH) could not be the exception. The Sheikh then turns to the "First Document" of Ali's appointment: the Day of the Warning (Yawm al-Inthar), where the Prophet gathered his relatives at the very beginning of his mission and declared Ali as his brother, executor, and successor. He highlights how this early appointment was deliberately distorted in works like Tarikh al-Tabari.

Let us look at the "Universal Logic." The Sheikh establishes a vital principle: **Every Prophet had a successor.** This is a historical and theological fact found in both the Quran and the narrations of all Abrahamic faiths. Adam had Seth, Noah had Shem, Abraham had Ishmael and Isaac, Moses had Aaron and Joshua, and Jesus had Simon Peter.

Is it logical that the "Seal of the Prophets," who brought the final and most perfect religion, would be the only one to leave his people in chaos? The **Reality of Religion** dictates that the succession of Ali was not a late political decision made at Ghadir Khumm; it was a **Primal Appointment** that began with the very first call to Islam.

We find the "First Paper" of this file in the incident of **al-Dha'wah al-Zul-Qurba** (The Call to the Near Kin). When the verse was revealed: "And warn your nearest kinsmen" [Quran 26:214], the Prophet gathered the children of Abd al-Muttalib. He offered them a deal:
Who among you will support me in this matter, to be my brother, my executor (Wasi), and my successor (Khalifa) among you?

The documents record that no one stood up except the youngest among them—Ali. The Prophet repeated the offer three times, and each time, only Ali stood up. At that moment, the Prophet placed his hand on Ali’s neck and said to the tribal leaders:
This is my brother, my executor, and my successor among you. Listen to him and obey him!

This was at the dawn of Islam, in Mecca, years before the migration. The appointment of Ali was the "Second Pillar" of the message after Tawhid. But look at what happened to this document. The Sheikh exposes the distortion in **Tarikh al-Tabari**. In some editions of his history, and especially in his Tafsir, Tabari changed the words of the Prophet.

Instead of recording the Prophet’s clear words: "my executor and successor" (Wasiyi wa Khalifati), they changed it in the printed books to: "He is my so-and-so and so-and-so" (Kaza wa Kaza). They tried to **erase** the appointment from the text itself! This is the **"Documentary Theft"** that has been practiced for centuries to hide the "Reality of Ali." They knew that if the people read the truth about the beginning of the mission, they would realize that the Caliphate was never a matter for "Shura" or election—it was a Divine Decree made from day one.
Segment 4: The Constant Education and the Position of Aaron

Sheikh al-Ghizzi continues to document the "continuous appointment" of Imam Ali (AS) throughout the life of the Prophet. He argues that the titles "Executor" (al-Wasi) and "Successor" (al-Khalifa) were names used for Ali in the presence of the Prophet. He counters attempts to narrow the scope of these words by presenting the logic that a Prophet’s words are part of a universal legislative program. He also discusses the "Position of Aaron" (Hadith al-Manzila) as a permanent rank.

The appointment was not a single event; it was a **Constant Education**. The Prophet (blessings of Allah be upon him and his family) did not just say "Ali is the Successor" once. He wove this reality into the very fabric of the community's life.

Look at the title **"al-Wasi"** (The Executor). In the Arabic language of that time, every tribal leader had an executor—the person who would settle his debts, fulfill his promises, and stand in his place. When the Prophet consistently called Ali "my Wasi," he was using a legal and social term that everyone understood as the **Designated Heir of Authority**.
We find reports even in the Sahihs where people asked:
Who is the executor of Muhammad?

The very fact that the question was asked proves that the concept of an executor was expected and known. But the "Reality of the World" tried to create a vacuum. They tried to claim the Prophet died "without making a will," which contradicts both his character and the Quranic command to every Muslim to make a will.

The Sheikh discusses the "Position of Aaron" (Hadith al-Manzila). When the Prophet was leaving for the Battle of Tabuk, he left Ali in charge of Medina. Ali, wanting the honor of Jihad, asked why he was being left behind with the women and children. The Prophet said:
Are you not satisfied to be to me as Aaron was to Moses, except that there is no Prophet after me?

This is a **"Legislative Snapshot."** Aaron was the brother, the partner, and the successor of Moses. By making this comparison, the Prophet gave Ali every rank of Aaron—leadership, authority, and the right to represent the Messenger—and only excluded Prophecy because the message was complete.

The critics say: "This was only for the duration of that battle." But the Sheikh explains the logic of the Message: Does a Prophet use the status of another Prophet [Aaron] as a "temporary job description"? No. When a Prophet defines a rank, he is defining a "Reality of Religion" that is permanent. This was the Prophet’s way of preparing the Ummah to see Ali as the "Aaron of the Muslims." To deny this is to deny the Prophet’s own clarity.
Segment 5: Documentary Theft and Academic Dishonesty

Sheikh al-Ghizzi deepens his investigation into the "Documentary Theft" performed by major historians. He focuses on the scholar al-Tabari, showing how the specific narrative of Imam Ali’s appointment during the "Day of the Warning" was systematically altered. The Sheikh explains that versions of his history were "cleansed" of the words "Executor" and "Successor" and replaced with "so-and-so." He argues this was a coordinated intellectual effort to prevent the "Reality of Religion" from challenging the "Reality of the World."
We must now look at the **Crime against the Text**. We mentioned the "Day of the Warning" (Yawm al-Inthar). This is one of the most documented events in early Islam, where the Prophet declared:
This is my brother, my executor, and my successor.

However, when we turn to the mainstream books of history, we see a miracle of "editing." Look at the work of **Muhammad ibn Jarir al-Tabari**. In his famous Tarikh (History), in the older manuscripts and his own earlier notes, the words are there. But as the centuries passed, the text changed.
The Sheikh details this distortion. In the widely printed versions of Tabari’s History, the Prophet’s statement is recorded as:
Who among you will support me... to be my brother, and kaza wa kaza (so-and-so and so-and-so)?
They literally replaced the titles Wasi (Executor) and Khalifa (Successor) with vague placeholders.

Think about the psychological effect on a reader. If a seeker of truth reads "so-and-so," they find no evidence. But if they read "my Successor," the entire structure of the Saqifa falls apart. This is why the Sheikh says the File of Ali is a struggle against **Academic Dishonesty**. They didn't just steal the leadership; they tried to steal the memory of the appointment.
The Sheikh notes that even the Great Sahaba (companions) were aware of this censorship. He cites the words of those who stayed loyal to Ali, who would say:
We heard the words of the Prophet with our own ears, yet the scribes of the Sultan write something else.

This is the logic of the World: If you can't kill the man, kill his titles. If you can't deny the event, corrupt the report. By turning Ali’s clear divine rank into an ambiguous "favor," they opened the door for people to claim that the Caliphate was a "political choice" rather than a "heavenly decree." The File of Ali is here to restore the "Executor" to his rightful place.
Segment 6: The Wasi - The ID Card of the Household

Sheikh al-Ghizzi presents documents showing a sermon by Imam Hassan (AS) in Sunni sources where he describes his father as the "Successor of the Prophet" (Wasi Rasul Allah). The Sheikh then moves to the battlefield of Ashura, showing that even in the most intense moments of conflict, the companions of Imam Hussain (AS) identified the Household through this title. This segment proves that the concept of the Wasi was a public identity recognized throughout the first century of Islam.

We must ask: did the companions and the following generation actually use these titles, or are they later inventions? The Sheikh provides a document from the books of the "other side." After the martyrdom of the Commander of the Faithful, **Imam Hassan** (peace be upon him) stood on the pulpit and delivered a monumental sermon.
In this sermon, which is narrated in the Musnad of Ahmad and other primary sources, Imam Hassan said:
Yesterday, there departed from among you a man whom those of old did not surpass in knowledge, and whom those of later times will never equal... he was the Successor (Wasi) of the Prophet.

Notice the precision. He didn't just say "my father" or "the fourth Caliph." He used the term that identifies **Ali’s Divine Link to Muhammad**. If this title were a lie, the people in the mosque—many of whom were companions—would have shouted in protest. Their silence was their admission of the "Reality of Religion."

Then we go to the most tragic document: the **Day of Ashura**. We look at the poetry and the war-cries (Rajaz) of the companions of Imam Hussain. When they stepped onto the battlefield to face the army of Yazid, they shouted:
I am the servant of the one who was the Wasi of the Prophet!
They identified themselves as the "Shi'a of the Wasi."

This proves that even fifty years after the Prophet’s death, the title "Wasi" was the banner of the Truth. It was the "ID Card" of the Household. Even the enemies acknowledged it. In their insults, they would say:
O you followers of the Wasi!

They used it as a term of derision, but in doing so, they confirmed the existence of the title. You don't insult someone by a title that doesn't exist. This shows that the **File of Ali** was always open in the hearts of the people. The blood of Ashura was shed to protect the meaning of this one word: **al-Wasi**.
Segment 7: The Private Confessions of the Abbasids

Sheikh al-Ghizzi explores how the title of "Wasi" remained a central point of contention even into the Abbasid era. He cites a letter from Muhammad ibn Abdullah (al-Nafs al-Zakiyya) to the Caliph al-Mansur. Furthermore, the Sheikh reveals a "Private Document" where the Caliph Harun al-Rashid admits to his son (al-Ma'mun) that the Imam of the time (Imam al-Kadhim) is the true owner of the Caliphate, and that the Abbasids are merely usurpers.

The truth of the **Wasiyya** (Succession) did not die with the first generation. It haunted the palaces of the tyrants for centuries. We find the letter of **Muhammad ibn Abdullah al-Mahdh** (known as al-Nafs al-Zakiyya) to the Abbasid Caliph al-Mansur.
In this document, Muhammad ibn Abdullah challenges the Abbasid's right to rule by reminding them:
Our father Ali was the Wasi (Successor) and the Imam... and the inheritance belongs to his children, not to the descendants of the uncle [Abbas].
This shows that the File of Ali was the legal basis for every movement that sought to restore Prophetic justice. Even those who disagreed on political tactics agreed on the identity of the Wasi.

But perhaps the most shocking document comes from **Harun al-Rashid** himself. The Sheikh recounts a narration where Harun was shown great respect by Imam al-Kadhim (peace be upon him). When the Imam left, Harun’s son, al-Ma'mun, asked:
O Commander of the Faithful, who is this man for whom you stood up and showed such honor?
Harun al-Rashid replied:
This is the Imam of the people, the Proof of Allah over His creation, and the Successor of His Messenger.
al-Ma'mun was stunned and asked:
If you know this, then why do you keep him in prison and sit on his throne?
Harun gave the ultimate definition of the **"Reality of the World."** He said:
O my son, power is 'barren' (sterile). By Allah, if you yourself were to struggle with me for this throne, I would take that which holds your eyes—your head—away from you!

This is a confession of the "Criminal Reality." They did not deny Ali’s succession because of a lack of evidence; they denied it because they loved the "Bride" (the World) more than they loved the Truth. They knew exactly who the Wasi was, but they chose to be "Kings" rather than "Believers." The File of Ali records these private confessions as the highest form of evidence against the usurpers.
Segment 8: The Original Sin – Mu'awiyah's Admission

Sheikh al-Ghizzi presents the correspondence between Muhammad ibn Abu Bakr and Mu'awiyah ibn Abi Sufyan. In this exchange, Muhammad ibn Abu Bakr names Imam Ali (AS) as the "Successor" (Wasi) of the Prophet. The Sheikh highlights how Mu'awiyah’s reply admissions that he and Abu Bakr were the first to "usurp" the right of Ali. The Sheikh then exposes how mainstream historians, once again led by al-Tabari, attempted to censor this letter from the records.

We turn now to a document that is like a lightning bolt in the middle of a dark night. This is the letter of **Muhammad ibn Abu Bakr to Mu'awiyah**. Muhammad was the son of the first Caliph, but he was raised in the lap of Ali, and he knew the "Reality of Religion" better than anyone.
In his letter to Mu'awiyah during the Battle of Siffin, Muhammad wrote:

Our master Ali is the Successor (Wasi) of the Messenger of Allah, the heir to his knowledge, and the first to believe in him... How can you, O son of the 'Liver-Eater' [Hind], compete with the one who was chosen by the Heavens?
Mu'awiyah’s reply is a **Document of Confession**. He wrote back saying:

We do not deny the excellence of the son of Abi Talib... indeed, we saw him with the Prophet like the stars around the moon. But your father [Abu Bakr] and his 'Companion' [Umar] were the first to snatch this matter from him. If they had not done so, we would not have disagreed with him today.

This is the **File of Ali** speaking through the mouths of the enemies. Mu'awiyah is telling the truth of history: the deviation did not start with him in Syria; it started in Medina at the door of Fatima. He is placing the "Original Sin" on the shoulders of those who created the Saqifa.

But look at the "Scholarly Treachery." The Sheikh reveals that when al-Tabari recorded this letter in his history, he did so with a heavy heart. In later editions, this letter was removed or heavily summarized to protect the "Sacred Image" of the early companions. They could not allow the Ummah to read a document where Mu'awiyah admits that the Caliphate was "snatched" from the Wasi.
The Sheikh connects this to the martyrdom of **Hijr ibn Adi**. Hijr was killed by Mu'awiyah because he refused to curse Ali. In the court of Damascus, Hijr stood firm and said:
I will never curse the one whom the Prophet called his Wasi.
This shows that the term "Wasi" was the red line. For the "Reality of the World," this word had to be erased.
Segment 9: The Broken Will and the Cost of Deviation

In the final segment of Episode 1, Sheikh al-Ghizzi concludes the "Document of the Will" by returning to Imam Ali’s Eulogy for Lady Fatima (SA) from Nahj al-Balagha. The Imam reports the "unification" of the Ummah in oppressing his daughter. The Sheikh ends with a powerful indictment of the "Majority" who chose to break the Prophetic Will, and concludes with a traditional Iranian salutation to Ali.

We reach the end of the first episode of the **File of Ali**. We have seen that the "Will" is a matter of instinct, a universal practice of Prophets, and a documented historical fact that even the enemies confessed to in their private letters.

But let us see the consequence of this "Broken Will." The Sheikh cites Sermon 202 from **Nahj al-Balagha**. This is the speech Imam Ali (peace be upon him) gave after he buried the "Leader of Women" in the darkness of the night. He stood at the grave of the Messenger and said:

O Messenger of Allah, peace be upon you from me and from your daughter who has come to stay in your vicinity... Your daughter will inform you of the 'Tadhafur' (unification) of your Ummah in oppressing her. Ask her the details, and let her tell you of the state of affairs.

Ali uses the word **"Tadhafur"**—meaning they gathered every ounce of their strength and unity, not to build the religion, but to "crush" (Hadh'm) the daughter of the Prophet. Why did they unite against a lone woman? Because she was the only one standing between them and the complete erasure of the Prophetic Will.
The Sheikh notes a narration from Imam al-Sadiq (peace be upon him) regarding the verse: "You are the best Ummah brought forth for mankind" [Quran 3:110]. The Imam said:
Is the 'best Ummah' the one that kills the Commander of the Faithful and Hassan and Hussain?
He explains that the "best Ummah" refers to the **Imams of the Progeny**.

The reason for all this blood and deviation is one thing: **They left the Will**. The "Wasi" was appointed by Allah, and the Prophet spent twenty-three years announcing him, but the "Reality of the World" could not bear the "Justice of Ali."

This is the end of Episode 1. Tomorrow, we will turn new papers. I leave you in the care of Ali and the love of Ali. I leave you with the greeting that the lovers of Ali in Iran use when they part. One says, **"Ya Ali!"** and the other replies, **"Ali Yaret!"**—which means "May Ali be your Helper." Ya Ali!

title: The File of Ali - Episode 2: The Miracle of Al-Ghadir
speaker: Sheikh Abdul-Halim al-Ghizzi

content.text: Overview: Episode 2

In this episode of The File of Ali, Sheikh Abdul-Halim al-Ghizzi explores the profound and inseparable connection between the Prophet's Will and the monumental event of Ghadir Khumm. He dedicates the episode to showcasing the overwhelming historical, literary, and theological evidence for Imam Ali's Wilayah, primarily by examining the encyclopedic masterpiece **Al-Ghadir** by **Sheikh Abd al-Husayn al-Amini**. The Sheikh highlights staggering statistical proofs compiled entirely from non-Shiite sources, noting that **110 Companions and 84 Successors** unequivocally narrated the event.

He demonstrates how early Islamic poetry by figures like Hassan ibn Thabit and Amr ibn al-Aas serves as undeniable historical documentation, and he reveals how prominent companions explicitly recited Quranic verses containing Ali's name. The episode concludes with a masterful theological debate by **Hisham ibn al-Hakam** in the court of Harun al-Rashid, logically proving the absolute necessity of an infallible Imam. Ultimately, Sheikh al-Ghizzi argues that the marginalization of Al-Ghadir is not due to a lack of evidence, but rather a **deep-seated, deliberate "Umayyad virus" of enmity** toward the Ahlulbayt.
Segment 1: The Will and the Miracle of the Al-Ghadir Encyclopedia

Sheikh al-Ghizzi opens the episode with a salutation to Ali, addressing the followers of Abu Turab.
