He establishes the thematic connection between the Will (Wasiyya) discussed in the previous episode and the event of Ghadir Khumm, stating that the Will was the opening of the Prophet's mission, while Ghadir was its conclusion. He then dedicates this segment to praising the monumental encyclopedic work, Al-Ghadir fi al-Kitab wa al-Sunnah wa al-Adab, authored by Sheikh Abd al-Husayn al-Amini. He describes it as a "miracle in the world of books," detailing the grueling, solitary 50-year effort Sheikh al-Amini undertook to compile it, producing a masterwork that even modern, well-funded institutions struggle to match in its breadth of sources.

In the name of Allah, the Most Gracious, the Most Merciful. Peace be upon the Radiant Face of Allah and His High Side. Peace be upon the ripe fruit and the brilliant star. Peace be upon the pleasing name of Allah, Abu al-Hassan Ali, and the mercy of Allah and His blessings. Peace be upon you, O followers of Abu Turab, in every corner of this existence—living and dead, young and old, men and women, elders and youth. Between your hands is the Alawi File, the second episode.

In the past episode, the talk was about the Will. In this episode, the talk is about the fragrance of the Ghadir. And **the Will and the Ghadir are inseparable.** The Will was since the first days of the noble mission, and the Ghadir was in the last days of the noble mission; a few days separate between the Ghadir and the departure of the Prophet, blessings of Allah be upon him and his family, from the world. The Will and the Ghadir are in one meaning, inseparable together. **The Will is an opening and the Ghadir is a conclusion.**

I talk to you today about the Ghadir, but my talk will be around the book of Al-Ghadir: **Al-Ghadir in the Book, the Sunnah, and Literature** by our Sheikh Abd al-Husayn al-Amini, may Allah the Exalted's pleasure be upon him. This encyclopedia for which we do not find a parallel in the world of books. Even within the limits of its currently printed parts—only the printed parts existing and available in the markets—I can say, with knowledge of what is in this book and with experience: **if the Shia have a miracle in the world of books in our present era**—for the Shia in general and for the seminary of Najaf specifically—if there is a miracle in the world of books, the encyclopedia of Al-Ghadir by Sheikh al-Amini is this miracle.

This talk, some may imagine it to be an exaggeration. Some may imagine it to be an amplification and magnification for a specific doctrinal matter. I say: everyone who has expertise in the arenas of investigation and in searching the bellies of books and tomes, all those who possess this specialization, if they faced the book of Al-Ghadir—even if they were not fair (I do not demand the fair ones here)—any human who has expertise in the world of investigation and in the world of books will stand a stance of astonishment before this tremendous effort—rather, more than tremendous—which Sheikh al-Amini expended.

And indeed, I am knowledgeable of the details of the life of Sheikh al-Amini and what there was in it of many unseen touches that were a helper to him and a companion to him in authoring this book. I will not point to them because I want to remain within the limits of ordinary logic for the talk, and for the research, and for dealing with the subject.

The book of Al-Ghadir by Sheikh al-Amini, of which what has not been printed is perhaps more than what has been printed now. The printed is eleven volumes. **That which has not been printed, perhaps if it were printed completely, it would reach to 22 parts, to 24 parts.** Meaning the printed is approximately half or less than half, and the rest is present with the heirs of Sheikh al-Amini, and the rest of the book was not printed. And despite that, the talk is with the printed Al-Ghadir.

Sheikh al-Amini expended an effort for a period of 50 years in authoring this book. **Half a century of time from his life**, from the life of this giant, Sheikh al-Amini, whose parallel is rare in research, and investigation, and comprehensiveness, and scientific honesty, and precision in transmission, and eagerness to transmit the opinion honestly, whether this opinion was opposing or agreeable to him. He reviewed, and scrutinized, and searched in very, very massive numbers of sources, and books, and tomes. And he traveled much in search of lines or pages in a book that reached him that he could find in a city that might be in the east or in the west. And he connected the work, his night with his day, until he produced this massive production: **Al-Ghadir in the Book, the Sunnah, and Literature.**

This is Al-Ghadir. These red parts, this collection of red parts, this is the encyclopedia of Al-Ghadir by our Sheikh al-Amini according to the known and available edition, according to the old edition. Sheikh al-Amini died in the year 1390 of the noble Hijrah, meaning before more than 40 years. Before more than 40 years. And he had published the book of Al-Ghadir between the forties and the fifties; in that period, the parts of the book of Al-Ghadir began to come successively, one following the other.

An institution in the city of Qom re-investigated and printed the book of Al-Ghadir, and it is the Al-Ghadir Center for Islamic Studies. And this part which is between my hands is the introduction which the institution wrote. This institution expended a great effort. This institution is under the supervision of the highest authority in the Islamic Republic, and many scholars, and investigators, and people of expertise, and those with experience in investigation and in following old and modern sources supervise it. This institution, the Al-Ghadir Center for Islamic Studies, expended a great effort, a wide and vast effort.

And despite the availability of all capabilities for this institution—this institution, as I said, works under the supervision of the highest authority in the Islamic Republic in Iran, and all means are available to it, and with modern technology, and the available capabilities, and the massive numbers of investigators and supervising scholars over this institution; with all these massive capabilities, and it expended a great effort—the investigators in this institution followed the sources of the book of Al-Ghadir and mentioned the sources which they followed in the last part which was printed as an introduction to the encyclopedia of Al-Ghadir, a chapter under the title "Sources of Investigation."

The sources of investigation which they reviewed reached to **928 sources.** You notice the massive number: 928 sources. And many of these sources have run out; they are not available in the markets and libraries easily, they have run out from the markets. And despite that, what do they say in the introduction? "And it is worthy of mention that what was established of these sources"—meaning these 928 sources—"does not exceed what was available of them and what was possible for us to reach. And there remained a number of them which we were not able to obtain."

Because there are many, many sources which this institution was not able—despite all its capabilities and despite all the effort which the workers in it expended—they could not reach all the sources which Sheikh al-Amini reached. And alone! By his personal effort, and who died before more than 40 years, without capabilities, and without technology, and without any supporting effort for Sheikh al-Amini. Rather, Sheikh al-Amini faced obstacles even in the Shiite milieu; he faced obstacles. And whoever knows the history of Sheikh al-Amini knows the details to which I pointed in a summarized manner. And **there were many campaigns demanding the destruction of the book of Al-Ghadir, its burning, and bringing Sheikh al-Amini to trial** in more than one state of the states which are called Islamic, meaning from the states in which the Muslims live.

And with all that, Sheikh al-Amini produced the book of Al-Ghadir, and what is in our hands is nothing but half of his massive effort which he expended. Therefore, I said that if the Shia in a general way, and the seminary of Najaf al-Ashraf in a specific way, have a miracle in the world of books, then their miracle is Al-Ghadir, the Ghadir of al-Amini.
Segment 2: The Staggering Statistical Evidence of Al-Ghadir

Sheikh al-Ghizzi delves into the first volume of the encyclopedia, highlighting the staggering statistical evidence compiled by Sheikh al-Amini to prove the authenticity of the Hadith of Al-Ghadir. He reveals that the book documents the transmission of the event by 110 Companions of the Prophet (starting with Abu Huraira) and 84 Successors (Tabi'un), utilizing sources strictly relied upon by Sunni Muslims. Furthermore, he emphasizes that 360 prominent Sunni scholars, spanning from the second to the fourteenth Islamic century, recorded this hadith. The Sheikh argues that this meticulous documentation exposes the historical suppression and oppression of the Ghadir narrative, challenging opponents to explain how they can rely on these same narrators for other Islamic laws while ignoring their testimonies about Ali.

I pass quickly over the book of Al-Ghadir by our Sheikh al-Amini, and I return to the old edition because this is the circulated edition. Perhaps the new edition which was issued by the Al-Ghadir Center for Islamic Studies is not available with everyone.

To begin the talk from the first volume of the parts of the book of Al-Ghadir. Certainly, I am not able to talk in this episode about the entire book, but rather I will talk in a summarized way about some of this book. And through this summary, the importance of the book of Al-Ghadir will become clear to the viewers, and also there will become clear to the viewers **something of the oppression of the Hadith of Al-Ghadir.** And I do not say from the oppression of Ali, for the oppression of Ali is vaster.

This is the first volume of the book of Al-Ghadir by our Sheikh al-Amini, may Allah illuminate his face, between the hands of the Master of Legatees. I point to the most important demands, and the first volume of the book of Al-Ghadir is packed with very, very massive information. Whenever I looked in the book of Al-Ghadir, whenever I leafed through the book of Al-Ghadir, this idea dominated me: **if there were an army of investigators, they would not have been able to gather this information which Sheikh al-Amini gathered**, may Allah the Exalted's pleasure be upon him.

From the topics that came mentioned in the first volume—this edition, as I said, is the old edition, and the edition between my hands is the edition of Dar al-Kitab al-Arabi, Beirut. I say the old edition in comparison to the new edition, otherwise its date, the date of this edition, is 1967, and it is the fifth edition.

On page 14: **the narrators of the Hadith of Al-Ghadir from the Companions.** And with very precise investigation with the books, and adjusting the names and the page numbers—to Allah belongs your goodness, O Sheikh al-Amini! The narrators of the Hadith of Al-Ghadir, and he arranges them according to the letters. So he begins with the first narrator from the narrators of the Hadith of Al-Ghadir: Abu Huraira al-Dusi. Abu Huraira, the narrator from whom the Sunnis transmit thousands of hadiths from the Prophet. Abu Huraira al-Dusi, and Sheikh al-Amini mentioned the sources and the books in which there exists what Abu Huraira transmitted from the Hadith of Al-Ghadir. He mentioned the books, the pages, and the authors. And I am not able to read everything; I want to pass quickly, and despite that, I will need a long time in order to pass quickly.

Page 14, Sheikh al-Amini enumerates the narrators of the Hadith of Al-Ghadir from the Companions. He begins with Abu Huraira, and the names continue. The second: Abu Layla al-Ansari. The third: Abu Zaynab ibn Awf al-Ansari. With the sources, the authors, the page numbers, the chains of narration, and all the details, until the talk reaches page 61. The last companion from the Companions who narrated the Hadith of Al-Ghadir: Abu Maraz Ya'la ibn Murrah ibn Wahb al-Thaqafi. And he mentions the memorizers, the hadith scholars, and the books that transmitted from him and from others. Every companion he mentions, he mentions the sources that transmitted from him and in detail. Do you know what the number of Abu Maraz is? **110. 110 from the Companions narrated the Hadith of Al-Ghadir.**

I said a short while ago, the one who plumbs the depths of this book, the oppression of Al-Ghadir becomes manifest to him. The oppression of the Hadith of Al-Ghadir. The oppression of the pledge of Al-Ghadir. **110 from the Companions** from whom the opponents of the Ahlulbayt narrate, with the mention of the sources present and relied upon by the opponents of the Ahlulbayt. All of them narrated the Hadith of Al-Ghadir. And these sources are present, the hadiths are present, and the books are present. 110 from the Companions, the first of them is Abu Huraira and the last of them is Abu Maraz. Sheikh al-Amini mentioned them from page 14 to page 61, the names and the sources. 110 from the Companions.

Then he mentions after that **those who narrated the Hadith of Al-Ghadir from the Tabi'un**—meaning the generation that came after the Companions. The Tabi'un are the generation who met the Companions and narrated from the Companions. Page 62 begins with the first one according to the alphabetical order: Abu Rashid al-Hubrani al-Shami. And he points also to the sources and to whoever authenticated him from the scholars of Rijal and from the scholars of Hadith with the details. Page 62, the first one: Abu Rashid al-Hubrani al-Shami. And according to the order of the letters of the dictionary, it continues to page 72. The last one: Abu Najih Yasar al-Thaqafi, deceased in the year 109 of the Hijrah. This is the last one from the list that Sheikh al-Amini established in the book of Al-Ghadir in the first volume. Do you know what his number is? **84. 110 from the Companions, 84 from the Tabi'un.** And all of these are respected and authenticated names, and the books that transmitted from them are known and present books, and the book is present; it is neither a fabrication from me nor a fabrication from Sheikh al-Amini.

After that, page 73 He mentioned 110 from the Companions, and among them are many of the known Companions and from the people of Badr. Among them is a group from the people of Badr. If there were ample time, I would have pointed to the names, but this needs a long time. The viewer can return to the book and see the detail of what I summarized of the speech. Then he mentions 84 from the Tabi'un. **Are all of these liars? If they were liars, then how do you transmit hadith from them?** And your books, and many of them are from the men of the two Sahihs. Many of these narrators, whether in the section of the Companions or in the section of the Tabi'un, al-Bukhari and Muslim relied upon them in the two Sahihs. 110 from the Companions—record these numbers—84 from the Tabi'un.

Classes of scholars: He begins from the second century, considering that the Companions and the Tabi'un approximately ended in the first century and a bit of the second century. So 110 from the Companions and 84 from the Tabi'un, these approximately continued to the ends of the first century and the beginnings of the second century. Therefore, the last one, as I said, died in the year 109 of the Hijrah, Abu Najih Yasar al-Thaqafi. Now he begins with the classes of scholars who transmitted the hadith from those Companions and Tabi'un. And he comes with a division **from the second century of the Hijrah to the fourteenth century.**

The first he begins with in the second century: Abu Muhammad Umar ibn Dinar al-Makki, deceased 115 or 116 of the Hijrah. Either 115 or 116. Mis'ar said: "He was trustworthy, trustworthy, trustworthy," as in the Khulasat al-Khazraji, page 244, to the end of the speech. He begins enumerating the second century. And after the second century comes the third, the fourth, the fifth, the sixth, the seventh, to the fourteenth century. This talk begins from page 73 to 147. This is the fourteenth century. The first of the scholars from the fourteenth century: Sayyid Ahmad ibn Zayni ibn Ahmad Dahlan al-Makki al-Shafi'i, born in Mecca, to the end of what he mentioned of the speech. And he continues in enumerating the scholars of the fourteenth century. He began from the beginnings of the second century to the fourteenth century, those whom Allamah al-Amini was contemporary with, to page 151.

The last name from the names of the scholars who narrated the Hadith of Al-Ghadir from the second century to the fourteenth century: The memorizer, the Mujtahid, the supporter of the Sunnah, Shihab al-Din Abu al-Fayd Ahmad, author of valuable compilations. He mentioned it in his majestic book Tashnif al-Adhan—meaning he mentioned the Hadith of Al-Ghadir, page 77—transmitting from a large group of the memorizers with chains of narration from 54 Companions. And they are: Ali, the Commander of the Believers... to the last one, Talhah ibn Ubaydillah. He mentioned the names of the Companions, 54 Companions from whom the supporter of the Sunnah Shihab al-Din Abu al-Fayd Ahmad, from the scholars of the fourteenth century, transmitted. And I here am bringing quick examples; the details are present in the book. Do you know what the number of this scholar is according to the sequence? **360 scholars from the most majestic of their scholars.**

Now what do we have? 110 from the Companions, and among them are many of the people of Badr and from the most majestic of the Companions. 84 from the Tabi'un, and they are the bearers of the hadith and the interpreters of the Quran and the most majestic scholars of the Muslims in that period. 360 from their senior scholars, from their senior hadith scholars, from the second century to the fourteenth century. You notice how much Sheikh al-Amini expended in reviewing the massive numbers of sources. True, this information is written in a specific number of papers, but **this information is not deduced from a book and from two books. This information requires reviewing hundreds and hundreds and hundreds of books** so that he could extract and bring them out with this extreme precision. 110 from the Companions, 84 Tabi'i, 360 scholars. This is one of them, Shihab al-Din Abu al-Fayd, transmitting the hadith from 54 Companions, and the names of the Companions are present here, mentioned.
Segment 3: The Adjurations and Sunni Authentications of the Hadith

Sheikh al-Ghizzi continues exploring the first volume of Al-Ghadir, detailing further overwhelming evidence collected by Sheikh al-Amini. He highlights that 26 authors (both Sunni and Shia) wrote entire books exclusively about the Hadith of Al-Ghadir. Furthermore, the book documents 22 historical adjurations (Munashadat) where Imam Ali and others publicly used Ghadir as an undeniable proof—such as the Day of Shura, the Day of Rahba, and the Day of the Camel against Talha—without anyone denying the event. The Sheikh then lists 43 major Sunni hadith scholars, including al-Tirmidhi and Ibn Abd al-Barr, who explicitly authenticated the hadith. He concludes by summarizing the staggering numbers derived entirely from Sunni sources, reiterating that this massive body of evidence exposes the profound oppression and deliberate marginalization of the Ghadir narrative and Imam Ali himself.

And did the matter end at this limit? Never. Another topic comes. Sheikh al-Amini holds a chapter: **The authors regarding the Hadith of Al-Ghadir**, from those who wrote about the Hadith of Al-Ghadir. Meaning they authored vast books only regarding the Hadith of Al-Ghadir, from the Sunnis and from the Shia, who authored large books. He begins with Muhammad ibn Jarir ibn Yazid ibn Khalid al-Tabari al-Amuli and ends with Sayyid Murtadha al-Khusrawshahi al-Tabrizi, the contemporary. **26.** Then he adds a completion to mention other sources on page 157, 158. **26 authors**, among them is he who is from the Shia and among them is he who is from the Sunnis.

The last one he mentioned, and he is from the Shia, Sayyid Murtadha al-Khusrawshahi. What does al-Amini comment on his book? He says: "He dedicated a book regarding the significance of the hadith," until he says: "He drew the bow to the utmost in the investigation." "Drew the bow to the utmost"—meaning he did not leave a stray or incoming detail, and there remained no pull in the bow. Sheikh al-Amini, when he says this speech, he knows what he wants and what he means. The rest of the books are also with this same description, with this same detail, whether it was a book from the Sunnis or from the Shia.

And does Sheikh al-Amini stop at this limit? He goes to hold a chapter for us under the title: **The Adjurations and the Protestations** (Al-Munashadat wa al-Ihtijajat). He traces the books, he traces the history and the biographies: in which place was the Hadith of Al-Ghadir argued with, and no one denied it? And when I say he argued with the Hadith of Al-Ghadir, not inside closed rooms, but rather either in open arenas or in a milieu of the elite of the people from the people of status.

Therefore, for example, the first adjuration: **the adjuration of the Commander of the Believers on the Day of Shura.** The day of the Shura of Umar, the council which Umar chose in the end of his caliphate, on page 160. And he transmits all of this not from Shiite sources, but from non-Shiite sources. So from among what the Master of Legatees said, addressing the sitting ones who convened the Shura, he said:

So I adjure you by Allah, is there anyone among you to whom the Messenger of Allah said: 'Whoever I am his master, then Ali is his master. O Allah, befriend the one who befriends him, and be the enemy of the one who is his enemy, and support the one who supports him, so that the present may convey to the absent,' other than me?

Meaning, did the Messenger of Allah say this to anyone other than me? They said: "By Allah, no." The conversation is long; I only took these lines in which there is the reference to the Hadith of Al-Ghadir.

And Sheikh al-Amini continues, mentioning the adjurations to us, like **the adjuration of the Commander of the Believers on the Day of Rahba in Kufa**, in the beginning of his caliphate, when some people demanded of him and asked him about Al-Ghadir and about the pledge of Al-Ghadir. So he gathered the people in the Rahba in Kufa and requested from the Companions who attended the event to testify. And there was a group of the Companions with Ali, prayers of Allah be upon him, in Kufa from those who attended the event and pledged allegiance to him, so they testified. And here he enumerates a group of their names, otherwise there are other names also mentioned in other sources.
