Meaning this is a proof of goodness! Of course, now they patch things up, they say because the companions of the Prophet, their concern was action. But action without knowledge has no benefit in it. Our Prophet himself says: "The superiority of the scholar over the worshipper is like my superiority over the lowest of you." He said... he said: "Two have broken my back: a reckless scholar, and an ignorant ascetic." The ignorant ascetic broke the back of the Prophet just as the reckless scholar broke his back. "Two have broken my back: a reckless scholar, and an ignorant ascetic." The ignorant ascetic, there is no value to his worship and to his action.

I do not believe that Ibn Abbas says this saying. Ibn Abbas, who says: "Ali narrated to me regarding the exegesis of the Basmala only." Ibn Abbas is the one who says, transmitting from the Commander of the Believers, that all the exegesis of the Quran is in al-Fatiha, and al-Fatiha is in the Basmala, and the Basmala is in the 'Baa', and the 'Baa' is in the dot, and I am the dot. He says: "Ali narrated to me from the beginning of the night to the rising of the dawn, to the morning, while he was explaining the Basmala. And he said: 'If I wished, I could load for you 70 camels from the exegesis of al-Fatiha.'" **Ibn Abbas, who understands the Quran and understands knowledge at this level, cannot praise the Companions because they did not ask the Prophet except about thirteen issues!**

And therefore, Umar ibn al-Khattab after that says, "I was distracted from it" when they ask him, O Commander of the Believers, when his ignorance appears in many of the issues. "Did you not ask the Messenger of Allah about that?" He says: "No." "Did you not hear the Messenger of Allah?" He says: "No." "What distracted you from that?" He said: **"The buying and selling in the markets distracted me from that."** Because he used to work as a broker, and this is present in their books, in the books of history and biographies.

The strange thing is that they fabricate this speech and attribute it to Ibn Abbas. Ibn Abbas praises the Companions because they did not ask the Prophet! And does this contain anything of praise or anything of wisdom? Then what are the questions that they asked? They asked about menstruation, about wine and gambling, about the new moons. Most of the questions were in practical issues that a human is afflicted with in his daily life. They did not ask him about beliefs, about deep knowledge, and they did not ask him about the Proof and about the direction to which the people return. Certainly they did not ask him, because they were in no need of a question, because the Prophet, blessings of Allah be upon him and his family, appointed Ali as a Legatee over this nation after him since the first days of the mission when the verse descended: "And warn your closest kindred." And the Prophet did not leave a situation except that he clarified this truth to them, to the last moment from the moments of his life was the pledge of Al-Ghadir.

And even on the day in which he was martyred, before he was martyred on Thursday, and the Calamity of Thursday is known. The Prophet was martyred on Monday. On Thursday, the issue of the Calamity of Thursday and the issue of the Book, and what Umar said that the Prophet is delirious, and this issue is known. What did the Prophet want to write in this Book? We have in our narrations: **He indeed wrote the book and gave it to the elite of the Shia of Ali, whom he said about that they are the successful ones: Salman, and Abu Dharr, and al-Miqdad.** This is present in our narrations. Just as Ali gathered the Quran and did not give it to the people; when he brought it to the people, they refused to take the Quran from Ali. Just as they refused the Quran of Ali, likewise they refused the book of Muhammad. Ali kept the Quran with him, and the Prophet, blessings of Allah be upon him and his family, wrote the book for the elite of the Shia of Ali. All of this is clarified in our hadiths transmitted from the Ahlulbayt, prayers of Allah and His peace be upon them all.

So this is the Quran, and this is their explicit statement that the Companions did not ask except these limited issues. And the historical reality, the reality... in tomorrow, the coming episode, God willing the Exalted, I will point to a group of witnesses, and incidents, and events that prove this process with a practical and scientific proof. Revealing to us what the Companions were upon of ignorance and of their lack of knowledge of the rulings and of the limits, which are from the axioms of religious knowledge, let alone the deep knowledge in Monotheism and in the secrets of the Quran! We do not demand of them the deep knowledge of Monotheism and the secrets of the Quran and its sciences; we talk about an issue of legal rulings, an issue of limits, an issue of the rulings of prayer. You find that the Companions do not know Tayammum, they do not know the rulings of doubts in prayer, they do not know the meanings of the Arabic words which the far and the near, the distant and the close know! They do not know their meanings while they recite them. They do not know the rulings of inheritance, they do not know, and they do not know, and they do not know! And the witnesses are not from Shiite books, nor from weak Sunni books. I will bring the witnesses from the considered Sunni books tomorrow in the coming episode. You will see the clear and explicit witnesses that talk about the lack of knowledge of the companions of the Prophet, blessings of Allah be upon him and his family.

As for Ali, he is unique, and Ali remains unique. No one can brand Ali with ignorance. No one can say that Ali was asked and did not know an answer to that question. **Ali is the gate of knowledge and the gate of recognition, and he is the gate of the city.** The gate of the city of knowledge, and the gate of the city of faith, and the gate of the city of wisdom. As for the speech of Al-Sawa'iq al-Muhriqah in that Abu Bakr is its foundation, and that Umar is its walls, and that Uthman is its roof, we will leaf through the books of the people tomorrow to see what is the foundation, and what are the walls, and what is the roof, from the books of the people, not from the books of others, and from the considered books. This is the first trait from the traits of the Imam and from the traits of the Caliph: Knowledge. I will not complete the speech on this topic in this episode; the completion of the speech comes to us continuously, and the episodes of this file support one another. The topics and the researches are intertwined, they come to us successively one after the other, God willing the Exalted.

This is the topic of knowledge. As for the topic of courage, is there any doubt regarding Ali, and that he is the bravest in the time of the Prophet, and before his time, and after his time, blessings of Allah be upon him and his family? Is there any doubt that he is the bravest of the people of his time? Does anyone doubt this truth? A clear issue, the Quran also states that explicitly if we wanted to go to the Quran. The Quran clarifies this truth to us. If we go to Surah al-Ahzab, and we read in Surah al-Ahzab, the tenth verse and what follows it:

When they came at you from above you and from below you, and when eyes shifted and hearts reached the throats, and you assumed about Allah [various] assumptions. There the believers were tested and shaken with a severe earthquake.

No one can say that Ali is in this verse: "When they came at you from above you and from below you, and when eyes shifted and hearts reached the throats, and you assumed about Allah [various] assumptions." Where is your certainty? Where is your faith while you think ill of Allah? "There the believers were tested and shaken with a severe earthquake. And when the hypocrites and those in whose hearts is disease said, Allah and His Messenger did not promise us except delusion. And when a faction of them said, O people of Yathrib, there is no stability for you, so return. And a faction of them asked permission of the Prophet, saying, Indeed, our houses are unprotected, while they were not unprotected. They did not intend except to flee. And if they had been entered upon from all its sides, then they had been asked to commit treason, they would have done it and would not have hesitated over it except briefly. And they had already promised Allah before not to turn their backs and flee. And ever is the promise to Allah that about which one will be questioned. Say, Flight will not benefit you if you should flee from death or killing; and then you would not be given enjoyment except for a little."

These verses talk about the Companions. They talk about fleeing, and about ill thought, and about fear, and about cowardice. What do these verses talk about? They talk about courage! Can anyone say that Ali is within these verses? That Ali is in this verse? "And Allah repelled those who disbelieved in their rage," just as we read a short while ago in their narrations, "they did not obtain any good. **And Allah sufficed the believers in fighting by Ali.**" They wrote thus in their books! Ali is in this verse, this is the verse of Ali. As for these verses: "And hearts reached the throats, and you assumed about Allah [various] assumptions," "and shaken with a severe earthquake," "Indeed, our houses are unprotected, while they were not unprotected, they did not intend except to flee," "And they had already promised Allah before not to turn their backs and flee," but they turned their backs! "Say, Flight will not benefit you if you should flee from death or killing."

Ali is not here. Ali did not pass from here. Everyone passed from here. Ali passed from here: "And Allah repelled those who disbelieved in their rage, they did not obtain any good. And Allah sufficed the believers in fighting"—He sufficed them by Ali. **"And Allah is Strong, Mighty."** A clear issue. Even if we wanted to turn away from these verses and narrations that passed, and the recitation of Ibn Abbas and Ibn Mas'ud, and these sources present in their books. If we read the history of the event, the event was completed and ended, and the victory came by the hand of Haydar, not by the hand of anyone else! O repeated attacker! O repeated attacker! On the day of the repeated attack, your essence knows all things, but it does not know the meaning of fleeing! O repeated attacker! O repeated attacker! On the day of the repeated attack, your essence knows all things, but it does not know the meaning of fleeing! Haydar, Haydar, O conqueror of Khaybar, indeed you do not know the meaning of fleeing, indeed you know the meaning of the repeated attack. Others know the meaning of fleeing. O repeated attacker on the day of the repeated attack! "And Allah sufficed the believers in fighting by Ali," prayers of Allah and His peace be upon him.

And the matter is not far when we go... when we go to Surah al-Tawbah, and we go to the 25th verse: **"And on the day of Hunayn, when your great number pleased you, but it did not avail you at all, and the earth, as spacious as it is, narrowed for you; then you turned back, fleeing."** And Ali is not here. Ali did not pass from here! "And the earth, as spacious as it is, narrowed for you; then you turned back, fleeing." Tomorrow we will talk about that repeated attacker who is not a fleer (Al-Karrar ghayr al-Farrar). We will talk, God willing, if there is time, about Khaybar, and about that repeated attacker who is not a fleer. Thus Muhammad, blessings of Allah be upon him and his family, described him. So that we may see who is the repeated attacker and who is the fleer. We will talk about Khaybar, we will leaf through history. There are many demands that are not said and not mentioned about the event of Khaybar; we will talk about them, God willing, if there is ample time tomorrow in the fourth episode. And if not, then in the fifth episode.

These verses are clear and explicit. Whoever wants to live in their atmosphere, the courage of Ali becomes manifest to him. "And Allah sufficed the believers in fighting"—He sufficed them by Ali, prayers of Allah and His peace be upon him. And I here do not want to deal with the historical events, and how the event of Al-Ahzab took place, and how what happened happened in the event of Hunayn on the day "you turned back, fleeing," O companions of the Prophet. The verse explicitly addresses the Companions! And some of the Companions reached in their flight until they arrived at Mecca. And this is mentioned in the books of the people, not in our books. In their books of hadiths, and history, and biographies.

Ali is the most knowledgeable, Ali is the bravest, and Ali is the infallible. Bring me one mistake in the history of Ali! When did Ali make a mistake? **Ali is a puzzle whose beginning is not known and whose end is not known.** Ali, whoever wants to look into his history will acknowledge his infallibility without the need to research in theology or research in the chains of narration of hadiths or the sources of hadiths. A look into the biography of Ali informs you of his infallibility. I do not want to talk here about infallibility; God willing, in another time, perhaps we will have a special file for infallibility. In another time, on another occasion, I will dedicate a special file to the topic of infallibility, because this is also a large topic.

Ali is the most generous. Who is more generous than Ali? "She said: So regarding whom did 'Has there come upon man' (Surah Al-Insan/Al-Dahr) descend in honor? May Allah the Exalted's pleasure be upon Al-Sahib ibn Abbad in his poem Al-Manaqib. He said: "She said: So regarding whom did 'Has there come upon man' descend in honor? I said: The most giving of the people of the earth for the supererogatory." He is the most generous, and he is the most knowledgeable.

Come with me, let us leaf through the Quran, just as we leafed through the Quran regarding the knowledge of Ali: "Say, Sufficient is Allah as a witness between me and you, and whoever has knowledge of the Book." Just as we leafed through the Quran regarding the knowledge of the Companions and their questions: "They ask you, they ask you..." this limited number of questions that reveals the extent of their knowledge. Just as we leafed through the verses in Surah al-Ahzab and in Surah Bara'ah about the flight of the Companions and about Ali, and the Quran tells us about his courage. Let us leaf through the Quran so that it tells us about the generosity of Ali. And who is more generous than Ali? **If generosity were a picture embodying itself on the face of this earth, this picture would be Ali, prayers of Allah and His peace be upon him.**

In Surah al-Baqarah, the 274th verse, verse 274: **"Those who spend their wealth by night and day, secretly and publicly, they will have their reward with their Lord, and there will be no fear concerning them, nor will they grieve."** The verse is present in our narrations and in the narrations of the people that it is regarding Ali, prayers of Allah be upon him. "Those who spend their wealth by night and day, secretly and publicly." He used to spend wealth by night and day, and secretly and publicly. His generosity was in all directions. His generosity did not take a specific form. The generosity of Ali in all times and in all modalities. The verse wants to point to this issue. "They spend their wealth by night and day," in every time, there is not a moment in which Ali is not generous. "By night and day, secretly and publicly," and in whatever modality it was, in the modality of secrecy and in the modality of publicness. "Those who spend their wealth by night and day, secretly and publicly."

In Surah al-Ma'idah, the 55th verse: **"Your guardian is only Allah and His Messenger and those who have believed, those who establish prayer and give Zakat while they are bowing."** Generosity even in the state of prayer and worship! Ali is generosity. If generosity were a picture, this picture would be dust under the feet of Ali. "And those who have believed, those who establish prayer and give Zakat while they are bowing." They spend by night—by night and day, and secretly and publicly. The verse passed a short while ago: "by night and day, secretly and publicly." The verse here talks even while he is in the station of worship, and Ali is in the station of worship, nothing distracts him from Allah, yet while he is bowing, his generosity is connected, emanating, because Ali is generosity, and generosity is Ali.
The verse in Surah... in Surah... in Surah al-Mujadilah. The thirteenth verse and the twelfth verse.

O you who have believed, when you privately consult the Messenger, present before your consultation a charity. That is better for you and purer. But if you find not [the means], then indeed, Allah is Forgiving and Merciful. Have you feared to present before your consultation charities? Then when you do not and Allah has forgiven you, then establish prayer...

to the end of the verse. This verse is known. This is a form from the forms of spending. Allah the Glorified and Exalted obligated the Muslims to pay money when they wanted to visit the Messenger of Allah, to converse with the Messenger of Allah. Where is the generosity of the Companions? All of them held back and did not pay! The only one who paid, pays the charity and comes to visit the Messenger of Allah, and this verse no one participated in with Ali, never.

This is the exegesis of al-Tabari. This is the twenty-seventh and twenty-eighth volume from the exegesis of al-Tabari, the known exegesis. In the tail of this verse: "O you who have believed, when you privately consult the Messenger, present before your consultation a charity." Page 50, page 25 from the twenty-eighth volume. With his chain of narration from Mujahid regarding His saying: "present before your consultation a charity." He said:

They were forbidden from privately consulting the Prophet until they gave charity. They were forbidden from privately consulting the Prophet until they gave charity. So no one privately consulted him except Ali ibn Abi Talib, may Allah be pleased with him. He presented a dinar and gave it in charity. Then the concession was revealed regarding that.
Another hadith from Mujahid, with his chain of narration from Mujahid. He said: "Ali, may Allah be pleased with him, said:

Indeed, in the Book of Allah the Mighty and Majestic there is a verse which no one acted upon before me, and no one will act upon after me: O you who have believed, when you privately consult the Messenger, present before your consultation a charity. He said: It was obligated, then it was abrogated.

**No one acted upon it from the Companions. Where is their generosity? Where is the generosity of the Companions?** The hadith books that they say about them, so-and-so spent such and such, and so-and-so freed so-and-so... these are things we do not acknowledge. We treat them with the treatment of chains of narration and sources as they want. As for this, it is Quran. Quran! And this is an established and known issue. This is a verse that talks about generosity, and about the lack of generosity of the Companions.

"O you who have believed, when you privately consult the Messenger, present before your consultation a charity." He said: "They were forbidden from privately consulting the Prophet until they gave charity. So no one privately consulted him except Ali ibn Abi Talib, may Allah be pleased with him." From Qatadah, he said: "The people asked the Messenger of Allah until they overwhelmed him, so Allah admonished them with this verse. And a man would have a need for the Prophet of Allah, so he could not fulfill it until he presented a charity before him. So that became severe upon them. Then Allah the Mighty and Majestic revealed the concession after that."

The verses are explicit. "Have you feared to present before your consultation charities?" Meaning have you felt pity for yourselves? Have you feared for your wealth, and did not feel pity for the Messenger of Allah? This is the meaning of the verse. "Have you feared to present before your consultation charities?" A visit to the Messenger of Allah, they could not pay a dirham? Is this the value of the Messenger of Allah to them? I do not know. The verse is clear and explicit. This is a situation. I do not say that the value of the Messenger of Allah to all the Companions in all times is like this, but this is a test. This is a test from the tests. It does not mean that the Companions did not have situations in which they had clear generosity; I do not deny these truths. There are truths present that we cannot deny of the generosity of the Companions in other situations. **But who is the most generous? The question is about the most generous, about the bravest.** I do not say that the Companions, all the Companions, were not brave. And I do not say that the Companions, all the Companions, did not have knowledge, never, I do not say this. But I search and talk about the most knowledgeable. Otherwise, we have in the narrations that Salman was given the first and the last knowledge. And there are among the Companions those who possess vast knowledge. But the talk is about the most knowledgeable. The talk is about the bravest. The talk is about the most generous. I do not say that the Companions had no generosity, but this verse reveals who the most generous is. The most generous is Ali.

The verses talked: he spends by night and day, secretly and publicly. He gives charity while he is bowing. When the command to give charity before private consultation descended, he is the one who gave charity, and no one else gave charity. All of this reveals the generosity. And the generosity of Ali does not stop at this limit. When we go to Surah al-Dahr or Surah al-Insan: **"And they give food in spite of love for it to the needy, the orphan, and the captive. We only feed you for the countenance of Allah. We wish not from you reward or gratitude."** This is generosity! We do not want reward or gratitude. We do not want a reward in exchange for this generosity, and we do not want thanks or praise. Why? Because generosity is an intrinsic quality in them, in the Ahlulbayt. An intrinsic quality in Ali and the family of Ali. And the intrinsic quality manifests without a cause, like perfume. Like a bottle of perfume: when the bottle of perfume is opened, the place fills with the good scent. Why? Because the nature of the perfume is that it spreads. The perfume here does not need praise; whether you praise the perfume or do not praise the perfume, the perfume spreads. The light of the sun, when it rises, it does not need you to say that this light is illuminating; it points to itself by itself.

"And they give food in spite of love for it to the needy, the orphan, and the captive. We only feed you for the countenance of Allah. We wish not from you reward or gratitude." And therefore, what is the reward for this generosity? **"Indeed, the righteous will drink from a cup whose mixture is of Kafur." "And they will be given to drink a cup whose mixture is of Zanjabil."** Until the verse says: **"And their Lord will give them a purifying drink."** This purifying drink is for whom? This purifying drink is for the purified essences. When we go to Surah al-Waqi'ah: "Indeed, it is a noble Quran. In a Register well-protected. None touch it except the purified." These are the ones who drink the purifying drink. And this noble Quran is their drink, it is this: "Indeed, it is a noble Quran. In a Register well-protected. None touch it except the purified." "And their Lord will give them a purifying drink." These purified ones, the purified ones drink the purifying drink. **Who gives them to drink? Their Lord.** Not the angels. "And their Lord will give them to drink." The cupbearer here is their Lord, is Allah. **"And their Lord will give them a purifying drink."** This is the drink of Ali and the family of Ali.

As for the drink of his enemies, in the same Surah al-Waqi'ah: "Then indeed you, O those astray [who are] deniers"—an address to the enemies of Ali, thus our Imams said, it is not I who says it—"will be eating from trees of Zaqqum, and filling with it your bellies, and drinking on top of it from scalding water, and will drink as the drinking of thirsty camels (Al-Hīm)." Al-Hīm: the thirsty, frantic camels. When the camels become frantic, they see the water, and they are thirsty, frantic, and agitated; they trample one another. "Then indeed you, O those astray [who are] deniers, will be eating from trees of Zaqqum, and filling with it your bellies, and drinking on top of it from scalding water, and will drink as the drinking of thirsty camels. That is their accommodation on the Day of Recompense." "And their Lord will give them a purifying drink." The exact same Surah. "Indeed, it is a noble Quran" in Surah al-Waqi'ah, "In a Register well-protected, none touch it except the purified." And the verses of the Book support one another, and the hadith of the Ahlulbayt with the verses of the Book support one another. The verses of the Book with the words of the Ahlulbayt produce for us the solid structure. Which structure is this? The structure of the Book and the Progeny. The Two Weighty Things: "As long as you hold fast to them." There is a solid structure, we build this solid structure from the Book and the Progeny, from the Two Weighty Things: "As long as you hold fast to them, you will never go astray after me."

This is the generosity of Ali, "we do not want from you reward or gratitude." You notice different referents, different pictures of the generosity of Ali, until the generosity of Ali reaches its most beautiful picture in the 207th verse from Surah al-Baqarah: **"And of the people is he who sells himself, seeking means to the approval of Allah."** Sells... sells! This verse descended regarding Ali when he spent the night in the bed of the Messenger of Allah, the night of the stay. "She said: So who is the one who spent the night upon the bed as a ransom?" And this is the courage of Ali also; this is his generosity and his courage here. "She said: So who is the one who spent the night upon the bed as a ransom? So I said: The most steadfast of Allah's creation in fear." "And of the people is he who sells himself, seeking means to the approval of Allah. And Allah is kind to [His] servants." This is the generosity of Ali, this is another picture from the pictures of the generosity of Ali. Until the clearest meanings of the generosity of Ali manifest in Surah Al Imran, in the 61st verse: **"Then whoever argues with you about it after what has come to you of knowledge, then say: Come, let us call our sons and your sons, our women and your women, our selves and your selves."** This is his generosity. The generosity with the soul is the utmost limit of generosity! Until he became a soul for the Messenger of Allah. This is generosity. This is generosity in a deep meaning. This is a generosity that we do not perceive. This is not generosity with wealth, nor generosity with the soul in the arenas of fighting. This is generosity of another meaning. This is the highest meaning of generosity! This is the generosity of Ali for Muhammad, and the generosity of Muhammad for Ali. Muhammad said: "I am Ali, and Ali is me," and Ali said: "I am Muhammad, and Muhammad is me." Peace be upon you, O you who are Muhammad, and Muhammad is you, O Master of the Legatees.

These are breezes from the fragrance of the Noble Book. And something of truths and documents. Clear, evident truths and documents from the books of those who were harsh to Ali, prayers of Allah and His peace be upon him, and turned away from him. Clear truths, as is written in the introduction of the program: irrefutable truths, and a screaming oppression. And I do not want to talk here about the oppression of Ali. **The oppression of Ali is greater than me, and greater than any speech.** The oppression of Ali, the Messenger of Allah knows it. The oppression of Ali, Ali knows it. Al-Zahra knows it. And therefore, what al-Zahra offered was a sacrifice in the arena of the oppression of Ali. Al-Zahra, prayers of Allah and His peace be upon her, was a sacrifice in the arena of the oppression of Ali. She is the witness, the martyr. And she is the first who defended the courtyard of Ali, the sacred courtyard of the Imamate.

The completion of the talk comes to us successively, God willing, in the coming episodes. Tomorrow, the same time, the same place, the same screen, and the same loyalty. Our appointment is renewed with you with Ali and the family of Ali, prayers of Allah and His peace be upon them all. I will meet you upon good. In the safety of Allah.

title: The File of Ali - Episode 4: The Ignorance of the Caliphs and the Valor of Ali
speaker: Sheikh Abdul-Halim al-Ghizzi

content.text: Overview: Episode 4

In this fourth episode, Sheikh al-Ghizzi contrasts the **absolute knowledge and peerless valor** of Imam Ali with the documented **ignorance and repeated flight** of the early Caliphs. Utilizing prominent Sunni sources like **Sahih al-Bukhari, Sahih Muslim**, and **Tarikh al-Tabari**, he exposes how Umar and Uthman were often unaware of basic Islamic jurisprudence regarding **Tayammum**, prayer, and gestation periods, necessitating constant correction from Imam Ali to prevent the execution of the innocent. Shifting to the battlefield, the Sheikh provides a rigorous Quranic and historical analysis of the battles of **Uhud and Khaybar**, revealing that while the Companions often fled in terror, Imam Ali remained the **singular, unwavering defender** of the Prophet. The episode concludes with a vivid account of the conquest of Khaybar, emphasizing that Ali’s superhuman feat of uprooting the fortress door was accomplished through **divine, Lordly power**, proving his exclusive fitness for the Imamate.
Segment 1: Hisham's Proof and the Ignorance of Tayammum

Sheikh al-Ghizzi opens the fourth episode by continuing the discussion on the logical proofs established by Hisham ibn al-Hakam, emphasizing that the true Imam must be the absolute most knowledgeable of all people. To demonstrate the contrast between the Imam and those who usurped his position, the Sheikh references the sixth volume of al-Amini’s Al-Ghadir. He presents a glaring example from Sahih Muslim and Sahih al-Bukhari detailing a profound incident of ignorance: Umar ibn al-Khattab, while serving as the Caliph of the Muslims, did not know the basic ruling for Tayammum (dry ablution) when a man asked him what to do if he was in a state of ritual impurity and could not find water. Umar's fatwa was simply "do not pray," contradicting the explicit text of the Quran.

In the name of Allah, the Most Gracious, the Most Merciful. Peace be upon the Radiant Face of Allah and His High Side. Peace be upon the ripe fruit and the brilliant star. Peace be upon the pleasing name of Allah, Abu al-Hassan Ali, and the mercy of Allah and His blessings. Peace be upon you, O followers of Abu Turab, in every corner of the corners of this existence—living and dead, men and women, young and old, elders and youth. Between your hands is the Alawi File, the fourth episode.

The talk is still continuous between two logics: between the logic of the clear truth, and between a logic that goes far away from the truth, between deception, and obscuring, and distortion, and alteration, and forgery. The speech is still in the folds of the argumentation of Hisham ibn al-Hakam which took place in the palace of the Abbasid Barmakid minister. And the speech reached with us to the most important traits of the Proof after the Messenger of Allah: infallibility, knowledge, courage, and generosity.

In the past episode, I spoke something about generosity, even though this demand requires more expansion, but the abundance of the titles that are between my hands makes me summarize in some of them and shorten some of the talk in some of its aspects. And the talk was also about knowledge, and that the Proof after the Messenger of Allah must be the most knowledgeable. He is the most knowledgeable of creation, let alone the Companions and the society of Medina. The Proof after the Greatest Prophet is the most knowledgeable of creation absolutely.

I dealt with the questions which the Companions asked and which the Quran mentioned, and which reveal to us the limitation of their knowledge. And I pointed to what came in the Noble Book in the last verse of Surah al-Ra'd regarding the absolute knowledge of Ali:
Say, Sufficient is Allah as a witness between me and you, and whoever has knowledge of the Book.

And I said that in this episode, meaning in the fourth episode, I will deal with some of the witnesses, and the scenes, and the referents that talk about the knowledge of those whom they said about that he is the most knowledgeable of the Companions: Umar ibn al-Khattab, Abu Bakr, Uthman, and their likes.

I point to models, and these are examples, and they are not upon the method of exhaustiveness and tracing, because exhaustiveness and tracing do not suit television programs. That matter is entrusted to encyclopedias and to the bellies of investigation. The talk passed in the second episode about the encyclopedia of **Al-Ghadir** which the pen of that giant, **Sheikh al-Amini**, may Allah the Exalted's pleasure be upon him, generously produced. In this episode, I brought this volume, and it is the sixth volume of the encyclopedia of Al-Ghadir according to the edition and investigation of the Al-Ghadir Center for Islamic Studies. I brought this volume of Al-Ghadir not because I do not possess the original sources, for some of them are present between my hands now, and the other part is present in my library. But rather, I wanted to make from the book of Sheikh al-Amini... I wanted to make Sheikh al-Amini a participant with us in this effort.

Sheikh al-Amini in this volume pointed to a group of witnesses and referents, and he extracted them from the mother books of the people. And I will take examples from that, and I will present texts from the sources of the people as an example that these events are present in their sources and to this hour. **Sheikh al-Amini mentions what Muslim extracted in his Sahih from the incident of the ignorance of Caliph Umar regarding the ruling of Tayammum.** And he mentions here the sources and the chains of narration which the scholars of the people mentioned in their books, and he detailed the saying in it in detail. This incident, I will read it to you from **Sahih Muslim**, and this is Sahih Muslim.

Sahih Muslim, according to the edition present with me, and it is the Dar Sader Beirut edition, on page 141, Hadith number 832. And if the editions are different, the hadith is in the Chapter of Tayammum. It is the fifth hadith from the hadiths of the Chapter of Tayammum. And according to the numbering of the edition present with me, 832. With his chain of narration:
That a man came to Umar and said: 'I became ritually impure (junub) and I did not find water.' So he (Umar) said: 'Do not pray.'
So **Ammar ibn Yasir** was present, and said:

'Do you not remember, O Commander of the Believers, when you and I were in a military detachment, and we became ritually impure and did not find water? So as for you, you did not pray. And as for me, I rolled in the dirt and prayed.' ... 'So the Prophet said: 'It was only sufficient for you to strike the earth with your two hands, then blow, then wipe your face and your hands with them.' So Umar said: 'Fear Allah, O Ammar.' He said: 'If you wish, I will not speak of it.'
Because the Caliph's mistake was exposed, so he did not want Ammar to clarify this matter.

**Al-Bukhari** in his Sahih, and it is a referent from the referents of deception with al-Bukhari. Of course, Dar Sader page 72, Hadith numbered 338, Chapter of the one performing Tayammum: does he blow in them? Meaning does he blow in his hands. With the exact same chain of narration: "A man came to Umar ibn al-Khattab and said: 'I became ritually impure and I did not reach water.' So Ammar ibn Yasir said to Umar ibn al-Khattab: 'Do you not remember that we were on a journey, you and I, so as for you, you did not pray, and as for me, I rolled and prayed. So I mentioned it to the Prophet, and the Prophet said: 'It was only sufficient for you this,' so the Prophet struck the earth with his palms and blew in them, then wiped his face and his hands with them."
And the speech ended. **The rest of the hadith is obscured!** "So Umar said: 'Fear Allah, O Ammar.' He said: 'If you wish, I will not speak of it.'"

This is Sahih Muslim, and this is Sahih al-Bukhari, and you notice the clear deficiency present in the hadith. And this is the method of al-Bukhari. This issue is not a strange issue; this is a normal issue in Sahih al-Bukhari, and it is the deception of the hadith and its cutting according to what is harmonious, according to what is agreeable to the praise of the Companions, even if the matter was contrary to that.
Segment 2: The Ignorance of the Caliphs Regarding Prayer and the Laws of Life and Death

Sheikh al-Ghizzi provides further shocking examples from Sunni sources exposing the Caliphs' ignorance of basic Islamic jurisprudence. He highlights how Umar ibn al-Khattab did not know what to do if one doubts in their prayer, resorting to asking a young Ibn Abbas. More disastrously, both Umar and Uthman ordered the execution by stoning of women who gave birth after only six months of pregnancy, completely ignorant of the Quranic verses that calculate the minimum gestation period. In Umar's case, Imam Ali intervened just in time to save the woman. In Uthman's case, Ali's intervention came too late, and the innocent woman was unjustly killed. The Sheikh concludes the segment with al-Amini's poignant question: Is it not a profound shame that men ignorant of the most basic laws of God were given authority over the blood and honor of Muslims?
Therefore, in Sahih Muslim: "I was sitting with Abdullah and Abu Musa upon this exact same method:

'What do you see if a man became ritually impure and did not find water for a month, what does he do regarding the prayer?' So Abdullah said: 'He does not perform Tayammum even if he does not find water for a month.'

Meaning he does not pray, because there is no prayer without purification. The exact same method, and it is the Umari method in the issue of not praying. And this arises from not knowing the ruling of **Tayammum**. And this is an everyday practical issue.

I ask you by Allah, if an Imam of a mosque in a neighborhood—I do not say an Imam of a grand mosque in the city, but a neighborhood from the neighborhoods, a district from the districts—if the Imam of the mosque does not know the rulings of his prayer, does not know the rulings of ablution, do the people pray the congregation behind him? So how do they say about Umar that he is the most knowledgeable of the nation? **And how can the Caliphate and the Imamate after the Messenger of Allah belong to him?** And these are their hadiths in Sahih al-Bukhari and in Sahih Muslim. And all the incidents to which I point, which Sheikh al-Amini mentioned, are present in these sources.

This is the first incident: ignorance of the ruling of Tayammum. The second incident: **The Caliph does not know the ruling of a doubt in prayer!** And this is a common practical issue that a human must learn. If the Imam of the Muslims does not know the ruling of a doubt in prayer, then what is the state of the Muslims? We here are not talking about deep sciences and vast divine knowledge; we are talking about simple meanings, about the ruling of Tayammum, about the ruling of a doubt in prayer. If these people do not know these matters... and these are truths present in their books.
This incident was mentioned by Ibn Hanbal in his Musnad and by al-Bayhaqi in the Sunan, and other sources... From **Ibn Abbas**, that Umar said to him:
"O boy"—because he was young in age—"O boy, did you hear from the Messenger of Allah or from anyone of his companions, if a man doubts in his prayer, what does he do?"

He said: "While he was like that, Abdul Rahman ibn Awf approached and said: 'What are you two doing?' So Umar said: 'I asked this boy, did you hear from the Messenger of Allah or anyone of his companions, if a man doubts in his prayer, what does he do?'" So the Caliph was ignorant of the ruling of the doubts of prayer. Is it permissible to pray behind an Imam who does not know the rulings of his prayer? And does an Imam rule the Muslims who does not know the axioms of the rulings?

The Caliph regarding the Book of Allah: The two memorizers Ibn Abi Hatim and al-Bayhaqi extracted from al-Du'ali that a woman was brought to Umar ibn al-Khattab. A judicial case was brought to him: **a woman who gave birth at six months. So he resolved to stone her!** She gave birth at six months. So that reached **Ali**, and he said: "There is no stoning upon her." So that reached Umar, so he sent for him and asked him. So he said:

Allah the Exalted said: 'And mothers shall suckle their children two full years' and He said: 'And his gestation and weaning is thirty months.' So six months of gestation and a full year [two years], so that is thirty months.

So he let her go. And Sheikh al-Amini mentioned the sources. **And this is a clear ignorance of the verses of Allah!** And which verses? The verses related to jurisprudential rulings, to the law. Meaning he is ignorant of the laws; a ruler ignorant of the laws.

And upon this exact same pattern... As for this narration or incident which I will point to now, it was extracted by Malik in the Muwatta, and al-Bayhaqi in the Sunan al-Kubra, and Abu Umar in Al-Ilm, and Ibn Kathir in his exegesis, and Ibn al-Dayba' in Taysir al-Wusul, and al-Ayni in Umdat al-Qari, and al-Suyuti in Al-Durr al-Manthur, and other sources.

From Abdullah al-Juhani, he said: "A man from us married a woman from Juhaynah, and she gave birth for him to a fully formed child at six months. So her husband went to **Uthman**"—in the days of the caliphate of Uthman. "So he ordered her to be stoned." The exact same knowledge! The exact same scientific level! "So he ordered her to be stoned.

So that reached Ali, so he came to him and said: 'What are you doing? That is not upon her.' Allah the Blessed and Exalted said: 'And his gestation and weaning is thirty months,' and He said: 'And mothers shall suckle their children two full years.' So the suckling is 24 months, and the gestation is six months; it is possible that it be six months. So Uthman said: 'By Allah, I did not realize this.'
So Uthman ordered her to be returned, **but found she had already been stoned!**

She was stoned, meaning she was killed... He said: "So the boy who was born at six months grew up. so the man acknowledged him, and he was the most resembling of people to him." He said: "So I saw the man later, falling apart limb by limb on his bed." Because the woman was killed unjustly by the ruling of the "just" Caliph Uthman ibn Affan, who was ignorant of the rulings of Allah.
Sheikh al-Amini has beautiful words commenting on this incident. He says:

Is it not a shame that the void of the Greatest Prophet is filled by people whose status in judgment is this? Is it from justice that men whose extent of knowledge is this are given authority over souls, honors, and blood? Is it from fairness that the Islamic laws, the rites of the nation, and the welfare of the Muslims are entrusted to the hands of caliphs whose biography is this?
Segment 3: The Caliph Ignorant of Simple Quranic Terminology and Laws

Sheikh al-Ghizzi provides yet another set of examples demonstrating Umar ibn al-Khattab’s profound ignorance. He recounts the famous incident where Umar tried to set a legal cap on women's dowries, only to be corrected publicly by an ordinary woman quoting Surah al-Nisa. Umar was forced to admit, "Everyone is more knowledgeable than Umar." The Sheikh then highlights the shocking story of Umar failing to understand the simple Arabic word "Abba" (pasture/fodder) while reciting Surah 'Abasa on the pulpit. Instead of seeking knowledge, Umar aggressively struck his cane and dismissed the inquiry as "unnecessary depth." The Sheikh notes the absurdity of the "most knowledgeable Companion" not knowing a basic word known to every Arab shepherd.
Another incident, and it is a known and famous incident, mentioned in many sources... Umar ibn al-Khattab mounted the pulpit of the Messenger of Allah, then he said:

O people, what is this excess in the dowries of women? The Messenger of Allah and his companions, the dowries among them were 400 dirhams and less than that... So I swear, I will not know of a man who increased the dowry of a woman over 400 dirhams.
He said, then he descended. "I will not know," meaning I will punish; if I come to know of it, if I know that anyone increased the dowry, I will punish him.
He said, then he descended. So a woman from Quraish objected to him and said:
O Commander of the Believers, did you forbid the people to increase the dowry of women over 400 dirhams?

He said: "Yes." She said: "Did you not hear what Allah revealed in the Quran?" He said: "And which is that?" She said: "Did you not hear Allah saying: 'And you have given one of them a **Qintar** (a great amount)'?" And the Qintar is a large amount of wealth. He said, so he said:
O Allah, grant forgiveness, all the people are more knowledgeable than Umar.

Then he returned and mounted the pulpit, so he said: "O people, I had forbidden you to increase the women in their dowries over 400 dirhams. So whoever wishes to give from his wealth, or whoever his soul is pleased, let him do so." These are simple issues in Islamic rulings! In the verses of the Noble Book! They do not need depth, and the Caliph is ignorant of them!

From Anas ibn Malik, he said: "Umar recited on the pulpit: 'And We caused to grow within it grain, and grapes and pasture, and olives and palms, and gardens of thick foliage, and fruits and **Abba**.'" He said: "All of this we know, so what is the Abba?" Then he flung a cane that was in his hand, he struck a cane that was in his hand, he moved it with a strong movement. So he said:
This, by the life of Allah, is over-burdening. So there is nothing upon you that you do not know what the Abba is.

And the Abba is the fodder of animals, the shepherds know it, the shepherds of the Arabs! All the Arabs know it. Al-Abba is the fodder of animals. And it is clear from the context of the verses... **Surah 'Abasa**, "He frowned and turned away... And caused to grow within it grain, and grapes and pasture, and olives and palms, and gardens of thick foliage, and fruits and Abba, as provision for you and your grazing livestock."

So he said: "This, by the life of Allah, is over-burdening. So there is nothing upon you that you do not know what the Abba is. Follow what its guidance has been clarified to you from the Book, so act upon it. And what you do not know, leave it to its Lord." How do we distinguish the things whose guidance has been clarified to us if the Caliph does not know the word Abba? What is the thing whose guidance has been clarified to the Caliph?
There is another narration transmitted from Umar ibn al-Khattab when he was asked about the Abba in these verses. So Umar said:
We were forbidden from depth and over-burdening.

Meaning this is from very deep knowledge, to know that the meaning of the word Abba is fodder! If this type of knowledge is from the "depth" that we were forbidden from, then what is it that we were commanded with then? So what knowledge do these Companions possess?
Segment 4: The Caliph's Disastrous Rulings on Life and Death

Sheikh al-Ghizzi presents a series of shocking historical incidents from Sunni sources demonstrating Umar ibn al-Khattab's profound ignorance of Islamic penal codes (Hudud). In multiple instances, Umar ordered the execution by stoning of women who were legally excused—including a mentally ill woman, a pregnant woman, and a woman coerced by extreme thirst. The Sheikh also highlights Umar's bizarre, invented ruling to amputate the hand of a thief based on his physical height (six spans), emphasizing that a Caliph who relies on such baseless ignorance and constantly requires Ali's correction is entirely unfit to lead the nation.

Another incident. From Ibn Abbas, he said: A mentally ill woman who had committed adultery was brought to Umar, so he consulted people regarding her. Certainly, he consults the Companions around him... and ordered her to be stoned. Meaning after he consulted, and they advised him to stone her, he ordered her to be stoned. So Ali passed by her and said: "What is the matter with this one?" They said: "The mentally ill woman of the tribe of so-and-so committed adultery, so Umar ordered her to be stoned." He said: "Return her."
Then he came to him and said: "O Commander of the Believers, did you not know, do you not remember that the Messenger of Allah said:
The pen is lifted from three: from the child until he reaches puberty, from the sleeper until he wakes up, and from the mentally ill until he recovers'?

So he did not go through with it. And Umar began to say Allahu Akbar... So he did not know the ruling, and the advisors who were around him did not know the ruling. What knowledge is this? And what caliphate is this? And what consultation is this? And what advisors are these?
Ali entered upon Umar, and suddenly a pregnant woman was being led to be stoned. He said: "What is the matter with this one?" She said: "They are taking me to stone me." So he said:
O Commander of the Believers, for what reason is she being stoned? If you have authority over her, you do not have authority over what is in her belly.
So Umar said: "Everyone is more knowledgeable than me," three times. This is the jurisprudence, and this is the law which the Caliph knew! A pregnant woman taken to be stoned!

Another incident. Umar ibn al-Khattab was brought a woman who married during her **'Iddah** (the waiting period)... So he took her dowry and placed it in the public treasury, separated them, and said: "They shall never unite." And he punished them both, he flogged them both... Because it is transmitted in another text: A woman who married during her 'Iddah was raised to Umar ibn al-Khattab, so he said to her: "Did you know that you married during the 'Iddah?" She said: "No." So he said to her husband: "Did you know?" He said: "No." He said:
If I knew, I would have stoned you both!

"If I knew, I would have stoned you both." On what basis? Was the woman married (Muhsanah)? Because stoning is for the married. If the woman was unmarried, how can she be stoned? ... So Ali said: "It is not like this. But rather this is ignorance from the people."

Another incident... Umar ibn al-Khattab sent for a female singer whom men used to enter upon... So while she was on the way, she panicked, and the labor pains struck her. So she entered a house and miscarried her child... So Umar consulted the companions of the Prophet. So some of them advised him: "There is nothing upon you. You are only a guide and a discipliner." ... So he turned to Ali and said: "What do you say?" He said:

If they said this by their opinion, then their opinion has erred. And if they said it to suit your whim, flattering you, then they have not advised you sincerely. I see that his blood money (**Diyah**) is upon you. For you terrified her, and she miscarried her child in your path...

So he ordered Ali to divide his blood money upon the **Aaqilah** (the tribe). So these are also the Companions around the Caliph. The Caliph is ignorant of the ruling, and the Companions, the advisors, are ignorant of the ruling too.

Another incident. Umar ibn al-Khattab was brought a woman who committed adultery. So she confessed, and he ordered her to be stoned. So Ali said: "Perhaps she has an excuse." Then he said to her: "What drove you to adultery?" She said: "I had a partner... and there was no water or milk in my camels. So I became extremely thirsty... But when I became extremely thirsty and I thought that my soul would depart, I gave him what he wanted, so he gave me a drink." So Ali said:
Allahu Akbar, whoever is forced without desiring or transgressing, then there is no sin upon him. Indeed Allah is Forgiving, Merciful.
Another incident, from Ibn Abi Mulaykah. This incident was extracted by Ibn Abi Shaybah, and Abd al-Razzaq... That Umar wrote regarding a boy from the people of Iraq who stole. So he wrote:
Measure him in spans (**Ashbiruh**). So if you find him to be six spans, then cut it.

Meaning, cut his hand. So he was measured in spans, and he was found to be six spans minus a fingertip. So he was left. There does not exist, neither in the books of the people, nor in our books, nor in any other books, that the sign of puberty is that a human spans a certain height! This is a ruling from his own inventions. It has no origin, not in the Sharia, not in the verses, not in the narrations...

Sheikh al-Ghizzi concludes his examination of the Caliph's lack of knowledge by citing Umar's own sermon at Al-Jabiya, where he publicly redirected questions about the Quran, lawful/unlawful matters, and inheritance to other Companions, reserving only matters of wealth for himself. Whoever wants to ask about the Quran, let him go to Ubayy ibn Ka'b. And whoever wants to ask about the lawful and the unlawful, let him go to Mu'adh ibn Jabal. And whoever wants to ask about the obligatory shares (**Fara'idh**), let him go to Zayd ibn Thabit. And whoever wants to ask about money, let him come to me, for I am a treasurer for it.

Meaning the Caliph does not know the Quran, does not know the lawful and the unlawful, and does not know the obligatory shares! Fara'idh means inheritance, a known terminology. Compare between this word and the word of Ali: "Ask me before you lose me!"
Al-Khatib extracted in Ruwah Malik, and al-Bayhaqi in Shu'ab al-Iman, and al-Qurtubi in his exegesis, with an authentic chain of narration from Abdullah ibn Umar, he said:
Umar learned **Surah al-Baqarah** in twelve years.

Surah al-Baqarah is Medinan, it was revealed in Medina. And the Prophet, all his stay in Medina was ten years. And we do not know when Umar began learning, and Surah al-Baqarah was not revealed from the first days. "Umar learned Surah al-Baqarah in twelve years. When he completed it, he slaughtered a camel." ... We do not know the extent of his knowledge of Surah al-Baqarah while he is ignorant of these simple things whose mention has passed.

These are the atmospheres. The atmospheres of the group that ruled the Muslims. These atmospheres... and Abu Bakr is not far from these atmospheres. Uthman, Abu Bakr, Umar. We shed light on Umar because they say that Umar is the most knowledgeable of the Companions. So if the most knowledgeable is in this state! And there are very many witnesses and incidents in the books of the people pointing to the ignorance of the Caliph regarding the simplest principles of Islamic rulings. So is it conceivable that the Proof after the Greatest Prophet, blessings of Allah be upon him and his family, is at this level of knowledge?

Sheikh al-Amini here, in this volume only... in this volume only! In this volume only, Sheikh al-Amini mentioned **100 incidents**. In this volume only, of the ignorance of the Caliph! The title of the chapter: "Rarities of Reports in the Knowledge of Umar." And this incident, the number of this incident is 100 on this page, here, where my finger points. The number of this incident is 100! He mentioned 100 incidents indicating the ignorance of the Caliph in the simplest types of Islamic rulings.

And he transmitted all these incidents from the books of the people, and not from one source, from tens and tens of sources. And he mentioned the sources, and mentioned the chains of narration. And all these incidents, by Allah, I have read them in their books, and I have read more than them.

Notice the logic with which the theologians in the school of the Ahlulbayt speak when they say, when Hisham ibn al-Hakam and his likes say, that the Proof who is appointed by Allah the Glorified and Exalted must be the most knowledgeable. Otherwise, if he is not the most knowledgeable, he will play with the destinies of the people, and with the blood of the people. And ignorance leads a human to incorrect action.
Segment 6: The Courage of Ali vs. The Flight of the Companions

Sheikh al-Ghizzi transitions to the next essential trait of the Imam: courage (Shuja'ah). He argues that if the Imam is not the bravest, he will inevitably flee from battle, leaving the nation vulnerable. Drawing exclusively from the Quran, the Sheikh recites numerous verses from Surah Al Imran, Surah al-Tawbah, and Surah al-Ahzab that explicitly condemn the companions for their panic, cowardice, and flight during the battles of Uhud, Hunayn, and Khandaq. He then contrasts their flight with the Prophet's famous declaration at Khaybar, where he promised the banner to Imam Ali, specifically describing him as a "repeated attacker, not a fleer" (Karrar ghayr Farrar). Finally, the Sheikh presents damning historical accounts from Sahih al-Bukhari and Tarikh al-Tabari, explicitly documenting that Uthman ibn Affan fled the Battle of Uhud and hid in the mountains for three days.

Courage (**Shuja'ah**): the bravest of people. Otherwise, if he is not the bravest, then he will flee, and the nation will flee with him. It is necessary that the Proof after the Greatest Prophet is the bravest. Let us pass over the verses of the Noble Book that talked about the flight of the Companions, and about their panic, and about their cowardice.
In Surah Al Imran, in the 144th verse:
And Muhammad is but a messenger. The messengers have passed on before him. So if he was to die or be killed, would you turn back on your heels?
This points to the flight of the people in the event of **Uhud** when the caller called out that Muhammad had been killed.
In the 152nd verse: "Until when you lost courage and fell to disputing about the order and disobeyed after He had shown you that which you love..." Until the 153rd verse says:
When you were climbing [the mountain] and paying no attention to anyone, while the Messenger was calling you from your rear.

"Climbing and paying no attention to anyone," fleeing! You were not looking back at anyone, and the Messenger was calling you from your rear: "O people, return! My companions, return!" And no one paid attention! The Quran is the one telling us, these are not narrations. "When you were climbing and paying no attention to anyone." You were not looking at anyone, you did not care about anyone.
And if we go to Surah al-Tawbah, Surah Bara'ah, in the 25th verse:
And on the day of Hunayn, when your great number pleased you, but it did not avail you at all, and the earth, as spacious as it is, narrowed for you; then you turned back, fleeing.

And in Surah al-Ahzab: "When they came at you from above you and from below you, and when eyes shifted and hearts reached the throats, and you assumed about Allah [various] assumptions. There the believers were tested and shaken with a severe earthquake." These verses are in the atmospheres of **Uhud**, in the atmospheres of **Hunayn**, in the atmospheres of **Khandaq** (The Trench), and in the rest of the battles.
Also in the atmospheres of **Khaybar**. The hadith of the Messenger of Allah, the known hadith:

I will surely give the banner tomorrow to a man who loves Allah and His Messenger, and Allah and His Messenger love him. This man is a repeated attacker, not a fleer (**Karrar ghayr Farrar**), Allah will grant victory by his hands.

And the Prophet used this description, he used the exaggeration form: Karrar (one who repeatedly attacks) and not just Kar (attacker). And he said ghayr Farrar (not a fleer). Why? Because the people who fled in the event of Khaybar were accustomed to fleeing!

If we wanted to go to Sahih al-Bukhari. This is Sahih al-Bukhari. Let us read in Sahih al-Bukhari: Book of the Virtues of the Companions of the Prophet, Chapter of the Virtues of Uthman ibn Affan... "A man from the people of Egypt came to perform Hajj at the House, and he saw a group of people sitting. He said: 'Who are these people?' They said: 'These are Quraish.' He said: 'So who is the Sheikh among them?' They said: 'Abdullah ibn Umar.' He said: 'O Ibn Umar, I am asking you about a thing, so tell me.
Do you know that Uthman fled on the day of Uhud?' He said: 'Yes.'
The question here is, what is the way in which Uthman ibn Affan fled? The speech here is summarized. Let us search in the books to see how the Caliph's flight from the battlefield was.

This is Tarikh al-Tabari. This is the first volume, Dar Sader edition, page 396. The events of the third year of the Hijrah: "And the people had been defeated from the Messenger of Allah..." The people, all of them were defeated without exception... "And Uthman ibn Affan, Uqba ibn Uthman, and Sa'd ibn Uthman—two men from the Ansar—fled until they reached Al-Jal'al in the direction of Medina towards Al-A'was, a very far area. So they stayed there for three days!"

Three days! They fled from the battlefield and remained terrified for three days! Then they returned to the Messenger of Allah. So what did the Messenger of Allah say to them? "You went away a broad distance." They claimed that the Messenger of Allah said to them: "You went away a broad distance" (meaning a very long distance). "You went away a broad distance"—meaning what kind of flight is this? Meaning you have scandalized yourselves with a great scandal. You fled, and for three days you were terrified, lost in the desert, in the mountains!
Segment 7: The Complete Flight at Uhud and the Disgrace at Khaybar

Sheikh al-Ghizzi presents a devastating account from Bihar al-Anwar where Abdullah ibn Mas'ud confesses that all the Companions—including Abu Bakr, Umar, Uthman, and Ibn Mas'ud himself—fled at the Battle of Uhud, leaving only Imam Ali to defend the Prophet, which prompted Gabriel's famous declaration: "There is no sword but Zulfiqar, and no youth but Ali." The Sheikh then transitions to the Battle of Khaybar, citing explicit, highly authentic Sunni sources like al-Hakim's Mustadrak, Kanz al-Ummal, and Tarikh al-Tabari. These texts uniformly record that Abu Bakr and Umar were each given the banner of the Muslim army, but both were completely routed, returning in disgrace and mutually accusing their own men of cowardice. The Prophet, pained by their repeated flight, then gave the banner to Ali, pointedly describing him as the "repeated attacker, not a fleer."

In **Bihar al-Anwar**, the exact same issue is mentioned. An issue present in the books of the Shia and in the books of the Sunnis. You noticed this is Sahih al-Bukhari and it contains the story of the flight of Uthman, this is the exegesis Al-Durr al-Manthur, this is Tarikh al-Tabari, this is the History of Ibn al-Athir. This is Bihar al-Anwar, the book of the Shia.

From Zayd ibn Wahb—this is the twentieth volume—from Zayd ibn Wahb: "I said to Ibn Mas'ud: 'The people fled from the Messenger of Allah, blessings of Allah be upon him and his family, until no one remained with him except Ali ibn Abi Talib, Abu Dujana, and Sahl ibn Hunayf.' So he said:
The people fled except Ali ibn Abi Talib alone!
Only him; even Abu Dujana fled and returned." Zayd ibn Wahb asks Ibn Mas'ud, he asks Abdullah ibn Mas'ud. And even Abdullah ibn Mas'ud, in the same narration, says: "I too fled."

"So I said to him: 'And where were Abu Bakr and Umar?' He said: 'They were among those who withdrew (fled) from the battle.' ... He said: I said to him: 'The steadfastness of Ali in that station is a wonder!' All this army, and Ali alone in the middle of this army! So he said: 'If you wonder at that, the angels wondered at it too! Do you not know that Gabriel said on that day, while ascending to the heaven:
There is no sword but Zulfiqar, and no youth but Ali'?

As for the event of Khaybar, I went to the event of Uhud to clarify the meaning of a fleer (Farrar), that the fleer is one who flees a lot. Uthman fled, Abu Bakr fled, Umar and the rest of the Companions, and by the text of the speech of Ibn Mas'ud—because this speech is from our sources, the people try to cut off the truths—no one remained except Ali ibn Abi Talib, only him alone, by the narration of Abdullah ibn Mas'ud! ... Khaybar was not conquered except by Ali ibn Abi Talib alone. Alone!

This is **Al-Mustadrak ala al-Sahihayn**. The Mustadrak of al-Hakim al-Nayshaburi, from the known books of the people... From Abu Layla, from Ali, that he said: "O Abu Layla, were you not with us at Khaybar?" He said: "Yes, by Allah, I was with you." He said:
For indeed the Messenger of Allah sent Abu Bakr to Khaybar. So he marched with the people, and he was defeated until he returned.

Hadith 4397: From Abu Musa al-Hanafi, from Ali... "The Prophet, blessings of Allah be upon him and his family, marched to Khaybar. So when he reached it, he sent Umar, may Allah the Exalted be pleased with him, and sent the people with him to their city... So they fought them, but they did not stay long until
Umar and his companions were defeated, so they came back making him a coward and him making them cowards.

Meaning he says to them, "You are cowards," and they say to him, "You are a coward." The Companions among themselves! The Companions led by Umar. Ali was not among them; if Ali had been among them, the balances would have differed.

And this is **Kanz al-Ummal**. This is Kanz al-Ummal by al-Muttaqi al-Hindi, and it is from the known hadith collections among them... From Buraydah: "So the Messenger of Allah sent Umar ibn al-Khattab with the people. So he met the people of Khaybar, but they repelled him and routed him." Routed him, meaning scattered him. "They repelled him and routed him." He fled from before them! Meaning he returned defeated, they defeated him.

This is the thirteenth volume of Kanz al-Ummal also... "And he sent Umar, but he was defeated with the people until he ended up with him. So the Messenger of Allah said: I will surely give the banner to a man who loves Allah and His Messenger, and Allah and His Messenger love him. Allah will grant victory to him. He is not a fleer (Laysa bi-Farrar)." He is not a fleer: an allusion to the fleeing people who fled from the battlefield.
Segment 8: The Poetry of Ibn Abi al-Hadid on the Flight at Khaybar

Sheikh al-Ghizzi quotes the famous poetry of Ibn Abi al-Hadid al-Mu'tazili, a prominent Sunni/Mu'tazilite scholar, to further illustrate the events of Khaybar. The Sheikh clarifies that despite his immense love for Ali, Ibn Abi al-Hadid was not a Shiite, explicitly warning viewers that his commentary on Nahj al-Balagha contradicts the Ahlulbayt's methodology. He then recites Ibn Abi al-Hadid's verses, which mockingly compare the fleeing Companions at Khaybar to frightened ostriches running from Marhab, while exalting Imam Ali as an immovable mountain and the true conqueror whose divine-like attributes bewildered human comprehension.
And the issue of the flight of the people from the battlefield is a known issue, a clear issue, and mentioned in poetry, in the poetry of poets across history.

These are the poems of **Ibn Abi al-Hadid al-Mu'tazili**. And Ibn Abi al-Hadid was not a Shiite. The opponents of the Ahlulbayt only describe him as a Shiite because he loved Ali... As for his commentary on **Nahj al-Balagha**, I advise our youth, I advise my sons and daughters against returning to this commentary, because it opposes the method of the Ahlulbayt 100%. Let some not mistakenly believe that the commentary of Ibn Abi al-Hadid is a Shiite commentary. This is a commentary opposing the Ahlulbayt, according to the Mu'tazilite methodology which opposes the method of the Ahlulbayt.
Ibn Abi al-Hadid, this litterateur and historian who is aware of the details of history, in one of his Alawite poems, the 'Ba'iyyah' poem with the 'damma', says:
And I do not forget, I do not forget those who advanced... and their flight, for fleeing has been known as a sin.

He is talking about the event of Khaybar. "And I do not forget, I do not forget those who advanced"—meaning they carried the banners and advanced, referring to Abu Bakr and Umar. A hub means a sin, a transgression. Fleeing! For whoever flees incurs the wrath of Allah. And therefore we say it is impossible for the Imam to be one who flees from the battle, because he would be in a segment of his life having incurred the wrath of Allah, even if he repented after that.

"And I do not forget, I do not forget those who advanced, and their flight, for fleeing has been known as a sin. And a great banner with which they went, with garments of humiliation and cloaks upon it. A tall, nimble one from the family of Musa repelling them..." He means he chases them away. Why did they flee? They were afraid of **Marhab** and of his brother al-Harith. "His sword and his spearhead spew death, and his scabbard and the spear shafts ignite fire.
Was their running, or the running of a frightened female ostrich?

He says: is this their running or the running of a female ostrich? Because ostriches are known for cowardice... So Ibn Abi al-Hadid says regarding these people who carried the banner: is this their running or the running of an ostrich? Meaning fleeing from Marhab. And are these men or women? "And are these two, or a soft-cheeked dyed one?"
Until he comes to the description of Ali, so he says... the poem is long, I am selecting verses from it:

A steed upon the back of the steed, and a great mountain, from which the mountains tremble in the duel... You embodied the actions of Lordship, by which whoever doubts that you are a servant is excused.

He points to a truth I will talk about. Taqayyalt meaning you wore, you assumed, you resembled the actions of Lordship. "You embodied the actions of Lordship, by which whoever doubts that you are a servant is excused."
Segment 9: The True Conqueror of Khaybar

In this final segment, Sheikh al-Ghizzi reads directly from Bihar al-Anwar, quoting the words of Imam Ali himself detailing the Conquest of Khaybar. The Imam describes how, after he killed Marhab, the fleeing Jewish forces locked themselves inside the massive fortress of al-Qamus. As the Muslim army remained behind the trench, Imam Ali single-handedly tore the colossal iron door off its hinges, using it first as a shield, and then famously bracing it against his own back to serve as a bridge over the trench for the Muslim cavalry to cross. He concludes by stressing the Imam's own testimony that he conquered Khaybar completely alone, aided by nothing but a "divine, Lordly power."

There remained a remnant from the event of **Khaybar**. We read the books of hadith, history, exegesis; we read types of speech, types of books. But I will capture clips from Bihar al-Anwar, from the hadith of the Ahlulbayt, from the books of the Shia of the Ahlulbayt, and they are more knowledgeable of what occurred in Khaybar. Clips, and not full details; the time is not enough, but they are clips. We talked about Ali, prayers of Allah be upon him, we talked about the bravest, we talked about that repeated attacker: "O repeated attacker on the day of the repeated attack, your essence knows all things, but it does not know the meaning of fleeing."

This is a clip from the hadiths which Sheikh al-Majlisi, may Allah the Exalted's pleasure be upon him, mentioned in Bihar al-Anwar, and this is the twenty-first volume of Bihar al-Anwar. "And when the Commander of the Believers, peace be upon him, killed Marhab..." This is a long and detailed hadith, and it mentions the defeat of the Companions. The hadith is transmitted from Al-Irshad of Sheikh al-Mufid, a long hadith over several pages, I took lines from it. "...And when the Commander of the Believers, peace be upon him, killed Marhab, whoever was with him returned." Whoever was with Marhab from the forces, from the army. "And they locked the door of the fort upon themselves." Marhab with his forces from which Abu Bakr fled on one day, then Umar on another day. Then came the repeated attacker, not a fleer, and he killed Marhab. When he faced Marhab, what were his words?
I am the one whose mother named him Haydara! I am the one whose mother named him Haydara, a lion of the thickets and a mighty lion. I measure out with the sword the measure of a sweeping wind.

Then he clove him in two. In the books of history, in the books of narrations, he clove him in two, such that when he was riding on the horse—what is placed on the horse? The saddle. A narration says that the sword of Ali descended upon the saddle and split the saddle in two halves also! Not only was Marhab split, Marhab was split in two halves, and the strike of the sword reached the saddle, to the saddle present on the back of the horse, splitting it in two halves also!

"So the Jewish knights who were with Marhab fled." There is a very important issue. The event of Khaybar is from the very exciting events, because it was not only the Jews in it, as is known. Especially in the books of the opponents, when they talk about the event of Khaybar, they try to diminish its status. This fort, the Fort of **al-Qamus**, whose door the Master of Legatees uprooted, this is the largest of their forts... But in our narrations, the fort was packed with men. From which men? From the Jews and from Quraish! And perhaps many do not know that Quraish was also participating in this battle, from the Jews, and from Quraish, and from the rest of the Arab tribes. And the speech will come.
"So the Commander of the Believers went to it, so he tackled it until he opened it. And most of the people were on the side of the trench, they had not crossed with him.
So the Commander of the Believers took the door of the fort and made it a bridge for them over the trench until they crossed.

In some narrations, in some narrations, the Prince, prayers of Allah and His peace be upon him, remained holding it until the armies crossed. A narration will come that he supported the door against his noble back, and the horses crossed, and the Muslims crossed over this door! Let him believe who believes, and let him disbelieve who disbelieves. These are our narrations and our hadiths. This is the repeated attacker, this is the non-fleer. This is our Ali.

On page 22, Zurarah transmits from Imam al-Baqir talking about the Commander of the Believers: "He reached the door of the fort, and it had been locked in his face. So he pulled it, a pulling that dragged it, and used it as a shield. Then he carried it on his back, and stormed the fort, an intense storming. And the Muslims stormed while the door was on his back!" Why? Because he supported it against his back so that the Muslims could cross over it. He made it like a bridge, like stairs, he supported it against his back. The Imam says:
So by Allah, Ali did not encounter from the people under the door more severity than what he encountered from the door!
Because they crossed with their horses, and he placed himself as a support for this large rock door so that the Muslims could cross into the inside of the fort!

On page 21, a narration from our Imam al-Sadiq from his fathers. This noble chain of narration, from al-Sadiq from his fathers, that the Commander of the Believers, peace be upon him, said in his letter to Sahl ibn Hunayf, and Sheikh al-Majlisi here transmits this letter from Sheikh al-Saduq:

By Allah, I did not uproot the door of Khaybar and throw it behind my back forty cubits by bodily strength, nor by nutritional energy. But I was aided by a Lordly power, and a soul illuminated by the light of its Lord. And I am from Ahmad like the light from the light.

The last thing I mention. Mentioned by Sheikh al-Majlisi on the seventh page. The Commander of the Believers says: "We arrived with the Messenger of Allah"—while he was conversing with one of the Jews—"We arrived with the Messenger of Allah at the city of your companions, Khaybar. Against men from the Jews, and what? And its knights from Quraish and others." So Khaybar was a battle not only with the Jews. The Jews, and Quraish, and other than Quraish.

So no one from them went out to me except that I killed him. And no knight stood firm against me except that I crushed him! Then I charged at them with the charge of a lion upon its prey, until I forced them inside the belly of their city, blocking the horizons upon them. So I uprooted the door of their fort with my hand, until I entered their city upon them alone! I kill whoever appears in it from its men, and I take captive whoever I find from its women. Until I conquered it alone, and I did not have any helper in it except Allah.

I ask you for supplication, O followers of the repeated attacker, not a fleer. I will meet you, God willing, tomorrow, the fifth episode. The same screen, the same appointment, the same loyalty. I leave you in the care of Ali. In the safety of Allah.

title: The File of Ali - Episode 5: The Ethics of the Caliphate
speaker: Sheikh Abdul-Halim al-Ghizzi

content.text: Overview: Episode 5

In this fifth episode, Sheikh al-Ghizzi conducts a rigorous examination of the **moral and ethical qualifications** required for the successorship of the Prophet. He contrasts the **sublime Prophetic ethics** of mercy, humility, and gentleness with the documented **harshness and systematic violence** characterizing the reign of the second Caliph, Umar ibn al-Khattab. Utilizing extensive evidence from canonical Sunni historical and hadith sources, the Sheikh details a **reign of terror** involving the arbitrary beating of Companions, domestic violence, and the violation of the **sanctity of the Prophet’s households**. This historical data is then placed against the **Shiqshiqiyyah and Qasi'ah sermons** of **Nahj al-Balagha**, establishing that while the Caliphate was turned into a **harsh enclosure** of blunt speech and stumbles, Imam Ali remained the **singular, flawless reflection** of the Prophet's divine discipline and character.
Segment 1: The Shiqshiqiyyah Sermon and the Harsh Enclosure

Sheikh al-Ghizzi opens the fifth episode by reviewing the established criteria for the Imam—infallibility, knowledge, courage, and generosity. In this segment, he focuses on the ethics and character of the Caliph, contrasting the "best of the Companions" (Umar ibn al-Khattab) with the Prophet Muhammad, who was sent to "perfect noble character." The Sheikh recites the Shiqshiqiyyah Sermon from Nahj al-Balagha, where Imam Ali describes Umar's reign as harsh, blunt, and filled with stumbles, likening him to a rider on a rebellious camel. He argues that a true successor must resemble the Prophet in humility, mercy, and gentleness, rather than in sternness and severity.

In the name of Allah, the Most Gracious, the Most Merciful. Peace be upon the Radiant Face of Allah and His High Side. Peace be upon the ripe fruit and the brilliant star. Peace be upon the pleasing name of Allah, Abu al-Hassan Ali, and the mercy of Allah and His blessings. Peace be upon you, O followers of Abu Turab, in every corner of existence, in every time and place—living and dead, young and old, men and women, elders and youth. Between your hands is the Alawi File, the fifth episode.

The talk passed in the first episode of the episodes of this file regarding the Will, and that the Will was since the first days of the noble prophetic mission.
